studyMetadata.title: Conversion
studyMetadata.subtitle: New not (just) Nice
studyMetadata.description:
A nine-session Bible study exploring what genuine conversion means – and why it matters.

Work through each chapter at your own pace, or use it with a group.
studyMetadata.author: Rick Creighton
studyMetadata.shareMessage:
Check out this Bible study app — “Conversion: New not (just) Nice”.

Available soon on Google Play and the App Store.

theme.theme-annotation: Nice — warm parchment and ink palette. Inspired by aged paper, calligraphy, and the tactile feel of a well-used study Bible. Earthy sepia tones with gold accent suggest careful scholarship and contemplative reading. Serious but inviting.

elements.text:
Suppose you were introduced to two men. Andrew is a man of strong moral standards – serious in his study of the Bible, a generous host, widely respected in his community. Ben is wealthy, but he has made his money from a combination of fraud, and barely legal exploitation of others. His neighbours avoid him.

But now suppose you find out that Andrew’s real name is Simon the Pharisee (Luke 7:36–50) and Ben’s real name is Zacchaeus (Luke 19:1–10). Simon was ’nice’. Zacchaeus was ’new’. Niceness is not the same thing.

elements.question: • Doesn’t ’conversion’ have negative connotations today?
elements.answer:
In some countries, the word conversion has become associated in popular culture with so-called ’conversion therapy’ – discredited attempts to change a person’s sexual orientation.


But the word itself is far older and richer than that misuse. In its biblical sense, conversion describes the most profound transformation a human being can undergo: a turning from darkness to light, from the power of Satan to God (Acts 26:18). It isn’t something done to a person against their will, and which that person regrets — it is something God does in a person, and which that person embraces.


This study uses the word in its full biblical sense. Genuine conversion isn’t coercion — it’s liberation.

elements.passageText: <sup>1</sup>Now a certain man, a Pharisee named Nicodemus, who was a member of the Jewish ruling council, <sup>2</sup>came to Jesus at night and said to him, ‘Rabbi, we know that you are a teacher who has come from God. For no one could perform the miraculous signs that you do unless God is with him.’ <sup>3</sup>Jesus replied, ‘I tell you the solemn truth, unless a person is born from above, he cannot see the kingdom of God.’ <sup>4</sup>Nicodemus said to him, ‘How can a man be born when he is old? He cannot enter his mother’s womb and be born a second time, can he?’ <sup>5</sup>Jesus answered, ‘I tell you the solemn truth, unless a person is born of water and spirit, he cannot enter the kingdom of God. <sup>6</sup>What is born of the flesh is flesh, and what is born of the Spirit is spirit. <sup>7</sup>Do not be amazed that I said to you, ‘You must all be born from above.’

elements.question: What does Jesus say is required to see the kingdom of God?
elements.answerPlaceholder: Remember to answer the question from the passage
elements.sampleAnswer: Jesus says that a person must be ’born from above’ (or born again) to see the kingdom of God. He further specifies being born of water and Spirit as the requirement to enter it. This is not a physical second birth but a spiritual one — something only God can bring about.
elements.questionHint: Look at Jesus’ direct reply in verse 3 — what condition does he set before anyone can even ’see’ the kingdom?

elements.question: Why is being ’born again’ (or ’born from above’) more radical than being more moral?
elements.answerPlaceholder: Remember to answer the question from the passage
elements.sampleAnswer: Jesus draws a sharp distinction in verse 6: ‘What is born of the flesh is flesh, and what is born of the Spirit is spirit.’ Moral improvement works on the flesh — it refines behaviour — but it remains in the same category. New birth is a different kind of change entirely: it is a new origin, a new nature, something only the Spirit of God can produce. Nicodemus, a deeply moral and religious man, is told this is not enough.
elements.questionHint: Notice the contrast Jesus draws in verse 6 — what does he say about the difference between what the flesh produces and what the Spirit produces?

elements.passageText: <sup>4</sup>Though I myself could have confidence in the flesh. If someone thinks he has good reasons to put confidence in human distinctions, I have more: <sup>5</sup>I was circumcised on the eighth day, from the people of Israel and the tribe of Benjamin, a Hebrew of Hebrews. I lived according to the law as a Pharisee. <sup>6</sup>In my zeal for God I persecuted the church. According to the righteousness stipulated in the law I was blameless.

elements.question: What religious qualifications did Paul have?
elements.answerPlaceholder: Remember to answer the question from the passage
elements.sampleAnswer: Paul’s credentials were exceptional by any religious standard: circumcised on the eighth day, born of the people of Israel, from the tribe of Benjamin, a Hebrew of Hebrews, a Pharisee in his observance of the law, zealous to the point of persecuting the church, and — by the law’s own measure — blameless.
elements.questionHint: Paul lists his credentials point by point in verses 5–6 — how many distinct qualifications can you count, and what do they cover?

elements.passageText: <sup>7</sup>But these assets I have come to regard as liabilities because of Christ. <sup>8</sup>More than that, I now regard all things as liabilities compared to the far greater value of knowing Christ Jesus my Lord, for whom I have suffered the loss of all things — indeed, I regard them as dung! — that I may gain Christ,

elements.question: How does he now regard them?
elements.answerPlaceholder: Remember to answer the question from the passage
elements.sampleAnswer: Paul now regards all those former assets as liabilities — worse than worthless. He uses the striking word ’dung’ (or ’rubbish’) to describe them compared to the surpassing value of knowing Christ. This is not false modesty but a radical revaluation: what once seemed gain has become loss in light of Christ.
elements.questionHint: Paul uses a very vivid and deliberate word in verse 8 to describe how he values his former credentials — what is that word, and what does its strength tell you about his new perspective?

elements.passageText: I regard them as dung! — that I may gain Christ, 9and be found in him, not because I have my own righteousness derived from the law, but because I have the righteousness that comes by way of Christ’s faithfulness — a righteousness from God that is in fact based on Christ’s faithfulness.

elements.question:
• What do you think of when you hear this word?
  • What do you think it really means?
  • Think of your own answer first, then look at ours.
elements.answer:
Most people assume conversion means adopting religious habits, cleaning up your behaviour, or having an emotional experience.


The Bible means something more radical: not self-improvement, but new life from God. Not a decision to be better – a resurrection.

elements.question: What does he say is the only true basis of righteousness?
elements.answerPlaceholder: Remember to answer the question from the passage
elements.sampleAnswer: Paul says the only true basis of righteousness is not his own law-keeping but the righteousness that comes through Christ’s faithfulness — a righteousness that originates from God himself. His own righteousness derived from the law is explicitly set aside as insufficient.
elements.questionHint: Paul sets up a direct contrast in verse 9 between two kinds of righteousness — what are the two sources he contrasts, and which does he say he wants to be found with?

elements.text: Niceness can smarten up the outside, but only new birth changes the heart. ’Conversion’ refers to this new birth. It is God making dead people alive. It includes turning from sin, trusting in Christ, and receiving new life from God.

elements.passageText: You know that I did not hold back from proclaiming to you anything that would be helpful, and from teaching you publicly and from house to house, 21testifying to both Jews and Greeks about repentance toward God and faith in our Lord Jesus.

elements.question: What two responses does Paul summarise as his message?
elements.answerPlaceholder: Remember to answer the question from the passage
elements.sampleAnswer: Paul summarises his entire message to both Jews and Greeks as calling for two responses: repentance toward God and faith in the Lord Jesus. These two are presented together as the core of what he proclaimed.
elements.questionHint: The verse names two specific responses Paul testified to — can you identify both, and notice to whom and in whom they are directed?

elements.question: How do repentance and faith relate to each other?
elements.answerPlaceholder: Remember to answer the question from the passage
elements.sampleAnswer: In the passage, repentance and faith are presented as two sides of one response — they are always named together and never separated. Repentance is turning away from sin and toward God, while faith is trusting in the Lord Jesus. They are not two separate stages but two inseparable aspects of genuine conversion: you cannot truly turn to Christ without turning away from sin, and genuine repentance naturally issues in faith.
elements.questionHint: Notice that Paul joins repentance and faith in a single summary statement — what does pairing them together suggest about how they relate to one another?

elements.passageText: 9For people everywhere report how you welcomed us and how you turned to God from idols to serve the living and true God and to wait for his Son from heaven, whom he raised from the dead, Jesus our deliverer from the coming wrath.

elements.question: What did the Thessalonians turn from – and to?
elements.answerPlaceholder: Remember to answer the question from the passage
elements.sampleAnswer: The Thessalonians turned from idols and turned to God — described here as ’the living and true God’. Their conversion had a clear direction: away from false objects of worship and toward the one real God, whom they now serve and await.
elements.questionHint: The verse uses the language of turning — what specific thing did the Thessalonians turn away from, and what (or whom) did they turn toward?

elements.question: What does this show us about the nature of conversion?
elements.answerPlaceholder: Remember to answer the question from the passage
elements.sampleAnswer: Conversion is directional — it always involves a turning from something and a turning to someone. It is not merely an internal feeling or a private decision; it is a public, observable change of allegiance. The Thessalonians’ conversion was so evident that people everywhere were reporting it. True conversion also results in active ongoing life: serving the living God and waiting for his Son.
elements.questionHint: Think about what the Thessalonians’ conversion looked like from the outside — what does the fact that ’people everywhere’ were talking about it tell you about what genuine conversion involves?

elements.passageText: <sup>17</sup>So then, if anyone is in Christ, he is a new creation; what is old has passed away — look, what is new has come!

elements.question: What happens to someone who is in Christ?
elements.answerPlaceholder: Remember to answer the question from the passage
elements.sampleAnswer: Paul says that anyone who is in Christ is a new creation. The old has passed away and the new has come. This describes a total transformation of identity, not a minor adjustment — a person in Christ belongs to a new order of existence.
elements.questionHint: Paul uses the language of creation, not renovation — what does the word ’new creation’ imply about the scale of the change that has taken place?

elements.question: How does this go beyond just change of behaviour?
elements.answerPlaceholder: Remember to answer the question from the passage
elements.sampleAnswer: Changed behaviour is a modification of what already exists. But Paul says ’the old has passed away’ and ’the new has come’ — this is not improvement of the old but its replacement. The category is creation, not reformation. A person in Christ is not a better version of who they were but an entirely new kind of person. Behaviour change can be achieved without God; new creation cannot.
elements.questionHint: If a house is renovated it still stands on the same foundation — but Paul uses the word ’creation’, and says the old has ’passed away’. What does that suggest about how deep the change goes?

elements.text: Conversion is not just a religious milestone, or an emotional experience, or a moral improvement plan. It is God making sinners new. And that only makes sense if something is deeply wrong with us – if the problem is serious enough to need it. It is. Our problem is not just bad habits – not just spiritual sickness, but spiritual death.

elements.passageText: <sup>1</sup>And although you were dead in your transgressions and sins, <sup>2</sup>in which you formerly lived according to this world’s present path, according to the ruler of the kingdom of the air, the ruler of the spirit that is now energizing the sons of disobedience, <sup>3</sup>among whom all of us also formerly lived out our lives in the cravings of our flesh, indulging the desires of the flesh and the mind, and were by nature children of wrath even as the rest…

elements.question: How does Paul describe our condition apart from Christ?
elements.answerPlaceholder: Remember to answer the question from the passage
elements.sampleAnswer: Paul describes our condition apart from Christ as spiritual death — dead in transgressions and sins. He adds that this deadness expressed itself in living according to the world’s present path, under the influence of a spiritual ruler (the devil), and driven by the cravings of the flesh. The conclusion is stark: by nature we were ’children of wrath’.
elements.questionHint: Paul uses three distinct phrases in verses 1–3 to describe what was shaping people apart from Christ — can you identify each of those three influences?

elements.passageText: 4But God, being rich in mercy, because of his great love with which he loved us, even though we were dead in transgressions, made us alive together with Christ — by grace you are saved!

elements.question: Who takes the initiative?
elements.answerPlaceholder: Remember to answer the question from the passage
elements.sampleAnswer: God takes the initiative. The verse begins ’But God’ — a deliberate contrast with the helpless condition described in verses 1–3. Since we were dead (and dead people cannot act), it had to be God who moved first. He does so because of his rich mercy and great love, not because of anything in us.
elements.questionHint: Given that Paul has just described people as spiritually dead, who is the only one who could possibly take the first step — and which two words at the start of verse 4 signal that?

elements.passageText: But God, being rich in mercy, because of his great love with which he loved us, 5even though we were dead in transgressions, made us alive together with Christ — by grace you are saved! —

elements.question: What does God do?
elements.answerPlaceholder: Remember to answer the question from the passage
elements.sampleAnswer: God makes us alive together with Christ — even though we were dead in transgressions. He brings spiritual life to those who had none. The verse summarises this simply: ‘by grace you are saved.’
elements.questionHint: The passage says God did something ’even though’ we were dead — what exactly did he do, and why is that action specifically the right response to death?

elements.question: Why is this described as ’grace’?
elements.answerPlaceholder: Remember to answer the question from the passage
elements.sampleAnswer: It is grace because it is entirely undeserved. The people being made alive were dead in transgressions — they had nothing to contribute and no claim to make. God acted purely out of his mercy and love, not in response to any worthiness in us. Grace is favour given freely to those who deserve the opposite.
elements.questionHint: Think about the condition of the people God acts toward in verse 5 — what does their state at the time tell you about whether they could have earned or deserved what God does?

elements.passageText: For the outlook of the flesh is death, but the outlook of the Spirit is life and peace,) 7because the outlook of the flesh is hostile to God, for it does not submit to the law of God, nor is it able to do so. 8Those who are in the flesh cannot please God.

elements.question: What is the mind set on the flesh like? What can it not do?
elements.answerPlaceholder: Remember to answer the question from the passage
elements.sampleAnswer: The mind set on the flesh is hostile to God — it is not merely indifferent or weak but actively opposed. It does not submit to God’s law, and crucially, it is not able to do so. Those in the flesh cannot please God. This is a condition of inability, not just unwillingness.
elements.questionHint: Paul says the mind set on the flesh ’does not submit to God’s law’ — but then he adds something even stronger in the second half of verse 7. What is that additional word, and what does it mean for any self-improvement effort?

elements.question: Why does this mean we need more than self-improvement?
elements.answerPlaceholder: Remember to answer the question from the passage
elements.sampleAnswer: Self-improvement assumes the self doing the improving is capable of the task. But Paul says the fleshly mind is not just unwilling — it is unable to submit to God, and those in the flesh cannot please God. If the very faculty we would use to improve ourselves is hostile to God and incapable of pleasing him, then trying harder within that same framework will not solve the problem. We need a different nature, not a better performance from the old one.
elements.questionHint: If the passage says we are not only unwilling but actually unable to please God in our natural state, what does that tell you about the kind of change that’s actually needed?

elements.text:
We do not need polishing. We need resurrection. Simon had everything that looked like godliness – and it counted for nothing. Zacchaeus had none of it – and was made new. Jesus did not come to make nice people slightly better. He came ’to seek and to save the lost.’

The question this leaves you with isn’t ’Am I a decent person?’ but ’Have I been made new?’ If you’re not sure, that’s worth spending time thinking about. The same Jesus who sought out Zacchaeus is not hard to find.
Questions for Further Reflection

elements.question: Think of someone who seemed very ’together’ spiritually – but whose life didn’t show evidence of conversion. What gave you that impression? What does that say about your assumptions?
elements.answerPlaceholder: Your reflections...

elements.question: Before doing this study, what did you think conversion meant? Has anything shifted in how you understand it? What feels most challenging or most encouraging about what you’ve discovered?
elements.answerPlaceholder: Your reflections...

elements.question: How do you know that you are truly converted? What would you point to – and what makes you uncertain?
elements.answerPlaceholder: Your reflections...

elements.text:
James had tried everything. He’d quit drinking twice – once for eight months. He’d ended the relationship that kept pulling him back into old patterns. He’d started going to the gym. He’d even started going to church, which surprised him as much as anyone. Each time, something changed for a while. Then, slowly, it didn’t.

He wasn’t a bad man. Everyone said so. He just couldn’t seem to become the person he wanted to be. And the older he got, the more he suspected the problem wasn’t his circumstances, or willpower, or bad luck. The problem was him.

James’ problems were real. But the Bible’s diagnosis goes deeper than any of them: our core problem isn’t that we’re struggling, or confused, or weakened by bad influences. It’s that we are, spiritually speaking, dead. Not just sick, but dead. Until we grasp this, conversion seems excessive. Once we grasp it, it’s the only thing that makes sense.

elements.passageText: <sup>1</sup>Have mercy on me, O God, because of your loyal love; because of your great compassion, wipe away my rebellious acts. <sup>2</sup>Wash away my wrongdoing. Cleanse me of my sin. <sup>3</sup>For I am aware of my rebellious acts; I am forever conscious of my sin. <sup>4</sup>Against you — you above all — I have sinned; I have done what is evil in your sight. So you are just when you confront me; you are right when you condemn me.

elements.question: Who does David say he has ultimately sinned against?
elements.answerPlaceholder: Remember to answer the question from the passage
elements.sampleAnswer: David says he has ultimately sinned against God — ‘Against you — you above all — I have sinned.’ This is striking because David’s sin (adultery with Bathsheba and the murder of Uriah) had clearly wronged other people too. Yet he recognises that the deepest dimension of every sin is vertical: it is an offence against God himself.
elements.questionHint: David acknowledges in verse 4 that his sin has a primary direction — read carefully who he says he has sinned against, and consider why that might seem surprising given what his actual sin involved.

elements.question: What does this teach us about the true nature of sin?
elements.answerPlaceholder: Remember to answer the question from the passage
elements.sampleAnswer: Sin is fundamentally a relational and vertical problem, not merely a social or moral one. David’s primary concern is not the harm done to Bathsheba or Uriah but the offence against God. Sin is rebellion against our Creator — it is setting our will against his. This is why sin is so serious: it is not just a personal failing but a personal affront to a holy God.
elements.questionHint: David also says in verse 4 that God is ’just’ to confront him and ’right’ to condemn him — what does his acceptance of God’s judgment suggest about who the wronged party ultimately is in every act of sin?

elements.text: Sin is not just wrongdoing. It is personal – it is rebellion against our Creator. It is not first about breaking rules. It is about rejecting God. That is a different kind of problem.

elements.passageText: <sup>9</sup>The human mind is more deceitful than anything else. It is incurably bad. Who can understand it?

elements.question: How is the human heart described?
elements.answerPlaceholder: Remember to answer the question from the passage
elements.sampleAnswer: Jeremiah describes the human heart as more deceitful than anything else and as incurably bad. It is so corrupt that the rhetorical question is asked: ‘Who can understand it?’ — implying that even we ourselves cannot fully see the depth of our own hearts’ corruption.
elements.questionHint: The verse uses two specific descriptors for the human heart — what are they, and what does the word ’incurably’ in particular suggest about what kind of remedy is needed?

elements.question: What warning does this give about trusting ourselves, or following our hearts?
elements.answerPlaceholder: Remember to answer the question from the passage
elements.sampleAnswer: If the heart is more deceitful than anything else and incurably bad, then following our hearts is not a path to wisdom but to self-deception. The very instrument we would use to evaluate ourselves is compromised. Our feelings, instincts, and moral intuitions are not neutral guides — they are shaped by a corrupted heart. This is a serious warning against the popular idea that we should ’trust ourselves’ or ’follow our hearts.’
elements.questionHint: The verse says the heart is ’deceitful’ — who is the heart deceiving, and what does that mean for the reliability of our own moral self-assessment?

elements.text: Sin runs deep, all the way through us. That’s not to say we are all as bad as we could possibly be – not at all. But no part of us escapes sin’s reach. It shapes what we want, what we think, how we see ourselves and others. The problem isn’t just our actions. It’s us.

elements.passageText: <sup>13</sup>And even though you were dead in your transgressions and in the uncircumcision of your flesh, he nevertheless made you alive with him, having forgiven all your transgressions.

elements.question: What was our condition apart from Christ?
elements.answerPlaceholder: Remember to answer the question from the passage
elements.sampleAnswer: Apart from Christ we were dead — specifically, dead in our transgressions and in the uncircumcision of our flesh. The phrase points to both the guilt of sin (transgressions) and our status as those outside the covenant people of God. Spiritual death is the diagnosis: not ill, not struggling, but dead.
elements.questionHint: The verse uses a very specific word to describe our state before God acted — what is that word, and how does it differ from simply being sick or weak?

elements.passageText: <sup>1</sup>And although you were dead in your transgressions and sins, <sup>2</sup>in which you formerly lived according to this world’s present path, according to the ruler of the kingdom of the air, the ruler of the spirit that is now energizing the sons of disobedience, <sup>3</sup>among whom all of us also formerly lived out our lives in the cravings of our flesh, indulging the desires of the flesh and the mind, and were by nature children of wrath even as the rest…

elements.question: What three influences shape our condition apart from Christ?
elements.answerPlaceholder: Remember to answer the question from the passage
elements.sampleAnswer: Paul identifies three shaping influences: the world (‘this world’s present path’), the devil (‘the ruler of the kingdom of the air’), and the flesh (‘the cravings of our flesh, indulging the desires of the flesh and the mind’). Together they describe a person trapped in a web of external pressure, spiritual opposition, and internal desire — all pulling away from God.
elements.questionHint: Look through verses 2 and 3 carefully — Paul uses three distinct phrases pointing to three different sources of influence. Can you identify one that is external and cultural, one that is spiritual and personal to an enemy, and one that comes from within ourselves?

elements.text: Perhaps the most confronting biblical description is this: we are dead. Dead people do not revive themselves. Conversion cannot be self-improvement. It must be resurrection.

elements.passageText: For the outlook of the flesh is death, but the outlook of the Spirit is life and peace, 7because the outlook of the flesh is hostile to God, for it does not submit to the law of God, nor is it able to do so.

elements.question: What is the mind set on the flesh like? Is it just weak, or something more?
elements.answerPlaceholder: Remember to answer the question from the passage
elements.sampleAnswer: It is something more than weak. Paul says the mind set on the flesh is hostile to God — that is an active posture of opposition, not passive weakness. It does not submit to God’s law, and — critically — it is not able to do so. This goes beyond struggling to obey: it is a fundamental incapacity rooted in a hostile orientation toward God.
elements.questionHint: Paul uses the word ’hostile’ — not ’struggling’ or ’failing’ — to describe the fleshly mind’s relationship to God. What does that word choice tell you about whether this is merely a weakness problem or something deeper?

elements.passageText: <sup>8</sup>Those who are in the flesh cannot please God.

elements.question: What is it unable to do?
elements.answerPlaceholder: Remember to answer the question from the passage
elements.sampleAnswer: Those who are in the flesh cannot please God. The verse states this as a flat impossibility — not ’find it difficult to please God’ or ’rarely please God’ but ’cannot please God.’ Any religious activity, moral effort, or self-improvement done in the flesh remains, by this measure, unable to satisfy God.
elements.questionHint: The verse does not say those in the flesh ’do not’ please God — it says they ’cannot’. What is the difference between ’do not’ and ’cannot’, and why does that distinction matter?

elements.question:
• What do you think of when you hear this word?
  • What do you think it really means?
  • Think of your own answer first, then look at ours.
elements.answer:
Wrath sounds like a loss of temper – God as an angry deity needing to be placated. But divine wrath is the settled, holy opposition of a good God to everything that destroys what he loves.


A God who was indifferent to evil would not be good. Wrath isn’t the opposite of love. In many ways, it’s the proof of it.

elements.passageText: For the outlook of the flesh is death, but the outlook of the Spirit is life and peace,) 7because the outlook of the flesh is hostile to God, for it does not submit to the law of God, nor is it able to do so. 8Those who are in the flesh cannot please God.

elements.question: Why does this rule out the possibility of us saving ourselves by our own moral effort?
elements.answerPlaceholder: Remember to answer the question from the passage
elements.sampleAnswer: Self-salvation by moral effort assumes that our moral effort is capable of reaching God. But the passage says the fleshly mind cannot submit to God’s law and those in the flesh cannot please God. If pleasing God is beyond us in our natural state, then no amount of moral striving from within that state can save us. The problem is not the quantity of our effort but the nature of our condition.
elements.questionHint: If someone is unable to please God in their natural state, what would more moral effort from within that same natural state actually achieve?

elements.text: Our problem is not just that we do not try hard enough. It is that, left to ourselves, we don’t truly want God. We can be generous, kind, even religious – and still be living entirely for ourselves. Self is the problem. And self cannot fix self.

elements.passageText: <sup>18</sup>For the wrath of God is revealed from heaven against all ungodliness and unrighteousness of people who suppress the truth by their unrighteousness,

elements.question: What is revealed from heaven? And against whom?
elements.answerPlaceholder: Remember to answer the question from the passage
elements.sampleAnswer: The wrath of God is revealed from heaven — against all ungodliness and unrighteousness of people who suppress the truth by their unrighteousness. The scope is universal (‘all’) and the reason is given: they suppress the truth. This is not an arbitrary anger but a just response to deliberate rejection of what is known.
elements.questionHint: The verse gives both a target (what kind of people) and a reason (what those people are doing) for God’s wrath — can you identify both, and what does the word ’suppress’ suggest about their relationship to the truth?

elements.question: Why is wrath a necessary part of God’s character?
elements.answerPlaceholder: Remember to answer the question from the passage
elements.sampleAnswer: God’s wrath is his settled, holy opposition to ungodliness and unrighteousness. A God who was indifferent to evil — who did not respond to the suppression of truth and the harm of injustice — would not be truly good. Wrath is the necessary counterpart of holiness and love: because God cares deeply about truth and about his creation, he cannot be neutral about what destroys them.
elements.questionHint: Think about a human judge who feels no anger at genuine injustice — would you call that judge good? What does your answer suggest about why a perfectly good God must respond to evil?

elements.passageText: And although you were dead in your offenses and sins, in which you formerly lived according to this world’s present path, according to the ruler of the domain of the air, the ruler of the spirit that is now energizing the sons of disobedience, 3among whom all of us also formerly lived out our lives in the cravings of our flesh, indulging the desires of the flesh and the mind, and were by nature children of wrath even as the rest…

elements.question: What does it mean to be ’children of wrath’?
elements.answerPlaceholder: Remember to answer the question from the passage
elements.sampleAnswer: To be ’children of wrath’ means to be people whose natural destiny — by virtue of what they are ’by nature’ — is to face God’s righteous judgment. It is not merely that God is occasionally displeased with us but that, apart from Christ, we belong to the category of those who are under his wrath. It is an identity and a status, not just a mood.
elements.questionHint: Paul says we were children of wrath ’by nature’ — what does the phrase ’by nature’ suggest about whether this is something we can change through effort or good intentions?

elements.question: Why is this a relational as well as a legal problem?
elements.answerPlaceholder: Remember to answer the question from the passage
elements.sampleAnswer: Being under God’s wrath is not just a legal verdict (guilty) but a relational rupture. We were living according to the world, the devil, and our own flesh — not toward God. The whole orientation of our lives was away from him. ’Children of wrath’ describes not just our legal standing before God’s judgment but a broken relationship with our Creator, who is also our Father by right of creation.
elements.questionHint: A legal problem can be fixed by paying a penalty — but Paul describes a whole way of life lived according to the world, the devil, and the flesh. What does that pattern of life tell you about the state of the relationship, not just the legal record?

elements.text:
The seriousness of sin is seen in God’s holy response to it. If God didn’t care about evil, he would not be good. His wrath is not uncontrolled anger – it is his settled, holy opposition to sin.

And yet. The same Bible that diagnoses us so honestly does not leave us there. The diagnosis is not meant to crush you – it’s meant to show you what kind of help you actually need. You don’t need to try harder, or think better of yourself, or make a few improvements. You need rescue. You need someone to do for you what you cannot do for yourself.

God has looked at our condition fully – and moved toward us anyway. That is where the next chapter begins.
Questions for Further Reflection

elements.question: Which aspect of the Bible’s diagnosis do you find hardest to accept about yourself – guilt, corruption, inability, or wrath? What makes that one feel most uncomfortable or most unreal?
elements.answerPlaceholder: Your reflections...

elements.question: Is there a type of person – or a category of sin – toward whom you find it easy to feel superior? What does the Bible’s picture of your own sin say to that?
elements.answerPlaceholder: Your reflections...

elements.question: In what ways are you tempted to minimise the seriousness of sin in your own life? Can you think of a specific sin you have been calling ’small’ – and what would it mean to take it as seriously as the Bible does?
elements.answerPlaceholder: Your reflections...

chapters.chapterTitle: God, who is Rich in Mercy

elements.text:
When Joy was seventeen, she ran away from home. Not dramatically – no argument, no slammed door. She just stopped coming back. New city, new name almost, new life. She told herself she didn’t need her family. That chapter was closed.

Years later, she found a letter. Her mother had been writing to her, every month, to the last known address. Every month for eleven years. Joy had never received a single one. She sat in her car in a car park and read through them in order. Her mother had never stopped looking. Had never assumed silence meant Joy didn’t matter. Had never, not once, written her off.

Joy had walked away. Her mother had never stopped reaching.

elements.passageText: <sup>4</sup>But God, being rich in mercy, because of his great love with which he loved us, <sup>5</sup>even though we were dead in transgressions, made us alive together with Christ — by grace you are saved!

elements.question: Why does God take the initiative to make peace with us?
elements.answerPlaceholder: Remember to answer the question from the passage
elements.sampleAnswer: The passage gives two reasons grounded entirely in God’s own character: he is ’rich in mercy’ and motivated by ’his great love with which he loved us.’ There is nothing in us that prompts his initiative — we were dead in transgressions. The cause is entirely in God: his mercy and love drove him to act toward those who could not act toward him.
elements.questionHint: Verse 4 gives two specific reasons rooted in God’s own character for why he acts — what are they, and notice that neither of them is anything to do with us?

elements.passageText: 9He is the one who saved us and called us with a holy calling, not based on our works but on his own purpose and grace, granted to us in Christ Jesus before time began, but now made visible through the appearing of our Savior Christ Jesus, who has abolished death and brought life and immortality to light through the gospel.

elements.question: Why (and when) did God choose to save us?
elements.answerPlaceholder: Remember to answer the question from the passage
elements.sampleAnswer: God saved us not because of our works but because of his own purpose and grace. And this was not a decision made in response to anything we had done — it was ’granted to us in Christ Jesus before time began.’ The timing is before creation itself, and the reason is entirely in God’s own gracious purpose, not in any quality or effort of ours.
elements.questionHint: The verse explicitly rules out one reason for God’s choice and names another — what does it say God’s decision was NOT based on, and what was it based on instead?

elements.text: God did not save us because he saw something promising in us. He saved us according to his own purpose – a purpose decided not just before we had done anything, but before we even existed. We don’t work our way to God. God moves first. His mercy is not a reward. It is a rescue.

elements.passageText: <sup>37</sup>Everyone whom the Father gives me will come to me, and the one who comes to me I will never send away. … <sup>44</sup>No one can come to me unless the Father who sent me draws him, and I will raise him up at the last day.

elements.question: Who comes to Jesus?
elements.answerPlaceholder: Remember to answer the question from the passage
elements.sampleAnswer: Everyone whom the Father gives to Jesus will come to him (verse 37). And from the other direction, no one can come to Jesus unless the Father draws him (verse 44). Together these verses show that those who come to Jesus do so because the Father has given them and drawn them — coming to Christ is not self-generated but is the result of the Father’s sovereign action.
elements.questionHint: Look at both verses together: verse 37 describes who will come, and verse 44 says what is required for anyone to come — what do these two statements together tell us about the role of human initiative versus divine initiative in coming to Jesus?

elements.passageText: <sup>15</sup>For he says to Moses: ‘I will have mercy on whom I have mercy, and I will have compassion on whom I have compassion.’ <sup>16</sup>So then, it does not depend on human desire or exertion, but on God who shows mercy.

elements.question: On whom does God have mercy? What does it depend on?
elements.answerPlaceholder: Remember to answer the question from the passage
elements.sampleAnswer: God has mercy on whom he chooses to have mercy — the decision belongs to him. Verse 16 makes the implication plain: it does not depend on human desire or exertion (our wanting or our working), but on God who shows mercy. Mercy is God’s sovereign gift, not a reward that humans can earn or qualify for by their striving.
elements.questionHint: Verse 16 names two things that mercy does NOT depend on — what are they, and what does it say mercy DOES depend on instead?

elements.text: Left to ourselves, we would not come to God. We wouldn’t even want to. But God does not leave us to ourselves. He comes for us.

elements.passageText: <sup>12</sup>But to all who have received him — those who believe in his name — he has given the right to become God’s children — <sup>13</sup>children not born by human parents or by human desire or a husband’s decision, but by God.

elements.question: Who are given the right to become children of God?
elements.answerPlaceholder: Remember to answer the question from the passage
elements.sampleAnswer: Those who received him — that is, those who believe in his name — are given the right to become God’s children. The right is given to them; it is not something they generate or possess by nature. Receiving and believing are the marks of those to whom this right is granted.
elements.questionHint: The verse uses two closely related descriptions of those who receive this right — what are they, and what do they tell you about what it means to be given the right to become a child of God?

elements.question: What three things does the new birth not depend on? What does it depend on?
elements.answerPlaceholder: Remember to answer the question from the passage
elements.sampleAnswer: The new birth does not depend on: (1) human parents (‘not born by human parents’), (2) human desire (‘not by human desire’), or (3) a husband’s decision. All three rule out any human origin or initiative for new birth. It depends entirely on God — ‘born by God.’ The new birth is wholly his work, not ours.
elements.questionHint: Verse 13 gives three ’not by’ clauses before it gives the one positive statement — can you list all three negatives, and then identify what the one positive source of the new birth actually is?

elements.passageText: <sup>18</sup>By his sovereign plan he gave us birth through the message of truth, that we would be a kind of firstfruits of all he created.

elements.question:
• What do you think of when you hear this word?
  • What do you think it really means?
  • Think of your own answer first, then look at ours.
elements.answer:
’Grace’ sounds like God being lenient – turning a blind eye, or grading on a curve. But grace isn’t God lowering his standards. It’s God acting in our favour when we had no right to expect it and nothing to offer in return.


It’s not a reward for the almost-good-enough. It’s a rescue for the lost. It is God’s free gift to us.

elements.question: Who brought us to new birth? And how?
elements.answerPlaceholder: Remember to answer the question from the passage
elements.sampleAnswer: God brought us to new birth — by his own sovereign plan, not ours. The means he used was ’the message of truth’, that is, the gospel. New birth is God’s sovereign act, accomplished through the proclaimed word. This rules out both self-generated spiritual rebirth and any mystical experience disconnected from the gospel message.
elements.questionHint: James identifies both the originator and the instrument of new birth — who willed it, and what was the specific means through which God brought it about?

elements.text: God doesn’t top up a spiritual life that was already flickering. He creates life where there was none. And he does it through the gospel — the message of Jesus Christ, spoken and heard.

elements.passageText: 1Now I want to make clear for you, brothers and sisters, the gospel I preached to you, that you received and on which you stand, 2and by which you are being saved, if you hold firmly to the message I preached to you — unless you believed in vain. 3For I passed on to you as of first importance what I also received — that Christ died for our sins according to the scriptures, 4and that he was buried, and that he was raised on the third day according to the scriptures, and that he appeared to Cephas, then to the twelve.

elements.question: What does Paul summarise as ’the gospel’?
elements.answerPlaceholder: Remember to answer the question from the passage
elements.sampleAnswer: Paul summarises the gospel as a sequence of historical facts about Jesus that he received and passed on as ’of first importance’: Christ died for our sins according to the Scriptures; he was buried; and he was raised on the third day according to the Scriptures. The gospel is not primarily a set of teachings or values but a report of events — death, burial, and resurrection — with a stated purpose: ‘for our sins.’
elements.questionHint: Paul calls certain things ’of first importance’ — how many distinct historical events does he list in verses 3–4, and what purpose does he attach to the first one?

elements.passageText: <sup>16</sup>For I am not ashamed of the gospel, for it is God’s power for salvation to everyone who believes, to the Jew first and also to the Greek.

elements.question: What is God’s power for salvation?
elements.answerPlaceholder: Remember to answer the question from the passage
elements.sampleAnswer: The gospel is God’s power for salvation. Paul does not describe the gospel as one helpful tool among many — it is the power of God for this purpose. And it is universal in its reach: to everyone who believes, to Jew and Greek alike.
elements.questionHint: Paul says the gospel IS something specific — not that it merely helps with something. What exactly does he say the gospel is, and for whom?

elements.passageText: <sup>17</sup>For the righteousness of God is revealed in the gospel from faith to faith, just as it is written, ‘The righteous by faith will live.’

elements.question:
• What do you think of when you hear this word?
  • What do you think it really means?
  • Think of your own answer first, then look at ours.
elements.answer:
The idea that Christ ’died for us’ is familiar enough to lose its force. It’s sometimes softened into Christ dying as a gesture of solidarity, or a demonstration of love.


But the Bible’s language is more specific: he died in our place, bearing the penalty that was ours. Someone else stood where we should have stood. That’s not just moving – it’s the hinge on which everything else turns.

elements.question: What is revealed in the gospel? And why do we need it?
elements.answerPlaceholder: Remember to answer the question from the passage
elements.sampleAnswer: The righteousness of God is revealed in the gospel. We need it because — as earlier chapters of Romans make clear — we have no righteousness of our own that is acceptable to God. The gospel is the place where God’s own righteousness is disclosed and made available. The quote from Habakkuk underlines the point: it is the righteous-by-faith who will live, not the righteous-by-works.
elements.questionHint: Verse 17 says a specific thing is ’revealed in the gospel’ — what is that thing, and given what you know from previous chapters about our condition, why would we desperately need it?

elements.text: The gospel is not advice about how to live. It is news about what God has done in Christ. We are called to speak it clearly. But the power to change a life is not in our delivery. It’s in the message itself.

elements.passageText: <sup>21</sup>God made the one who did not know sin to be sin for us, so that in him we would become the righteousness of God.

elements.question: What did God make Christ to be?
elements.answerPlaceholder: Remember to answer the question from the passage
elements.sampleAnswer: God made Christ — the one who did not know sin — to be sin for us. This is the great exchange at the heart of the gospel: the sinless one was treated as sin, so that we might become the righteousness of God in him. God did not overlook sin; he dealt with it by placing it on Christ.
elements.questionHint: The verse describes a remarkable exchange — what was Christ made to be, and what do we become as a result? How do these two sides of the exchange correspond to each other?

elements.question: What does this teach us about substitution and salvation?
elements.answerPlaceholder: Remember to answer the question from the passage
elements.sampleAnswer: Substitution means that Christ took our place — he bore what we deserved (being treated as sin) so that we might receive what he deserved (the righteousness of God). Salvation is not God declaring us righteous on the basis of our own efforts but on the basis of what Christ did in our place. The whole verse is structured around this swap: his condemnation for our justification.
elements.questionHint: Christ ’did not know sin’ — he had no sin of his own. So why does the verse say God made him ’to be sin’? Who does that suggest Christ was standing in for, and what does that mean for us?

elements.passageText: <sup>18</sup>Because Christ also suffered once for sins, the just for the unjust, to bring you to God, by being put to death in the flesh but by being made alive in the spirit.

elements.question: Why did Christ suffer? And who for?
elements.answerPlaceholder: Remember to answer the question from the passage
elements.sampleAnswer: Christ suffered for sins — to deal with the problem of sin once and for all (‘once’). He suffered as the just for the unjust — in our place, the righteous one taking what the unrighteous deserved. The stated goal is ’to bring you to God.’ His suffering was not aimless; it had a relational purpose: restoring the broken relationship between sinners and God.
elements.questionHint: Peter gives three pieces of information about Christ’s suffering: how many times, in whose place, and for what purpose. Can you find all three — and which one tells you about the ultimate goal of the cross?

elements.text: Conversion happens because Christ has done all that’s needed. We don’t convert ourselves into God’s favour, we don’t earn it. We trust in the One who has secured it. The work is done. We receive it.

elements.passageText: <sup>30</sup>Therefore, although God has overlooked such times of ignorance, he now commands all people everywhere to repent, <sup>31</sup>because he has set a day on which he is going to judge the world in righteousness, by a man whom he designated, having provided proof to everyone by raising him from the dead.

elements.question: What does God now command? And why is it urgent?
elements.answerPlaceholder: Remember to answer the question from the passage
elements.sampleAnswer: God now commands all people everywhere to repent. The urgency is grounded in what is coming: God has set a day on which he will judge the world in righteousness, through the man he has designated — a claim confirmed by the resurrection. Repentance is urgent because the judgment is certain, the Judge is appointed, and the proof of his identity and authority has already been given.
elements.questionHint: The word ’because’ in verse 31 connects the command with a reason — what is that reason, and why does the resurrection make the urgency of repentance greater rather than smaller?

elements.passageText: <sup>14</sup>How are they to call on one they have not believed in? And how are they to believe in one they have not heard of? And how are they to hear without someone preaching to them? <sup>15</sup>And how are they to preach unless they are sent? As it is written, ‘How timely is the arrival of those who proclaim the good news.’ <sup>16</sup>But not all have obeyed the good news, for Isaiah says, ‘Lord, who has believed our report?’ <sup>17</sup>Consequently faith comes from what is heard, and what is heard comes through the preached word of Christ.

elements.question: How does someone come to believe?
elements.answerPlaceholder: Remember to answer the question from the passage
elements.sampleAnswer: Faith comes from what is heard, and what is heard comes through the preached word of Christ (verse 17). Paul traces the chain backward: calling on Christ requires believing; believing requires hearing; hearing requires someone preaching; preaching requires being sent. The ordinary means of coming to faith is hearing the proclaimed gospel — there is no shortcut that bypasses the word of Christ being spoken and received.
elements.questionHint: Paul uses a chain of rhetorical questions in verses 14–15 — try following the chain in reverse from verse 17 backwards. What does that chain tell you about the necessary steps between someone being sent and someone finally having faith?

elements.text:
None of this means we are simply passive. God’s grace doesn’t bypass us – it reaches us. And when it reaches us, it calls for a real response: repentance and faith. Conversion is not the result of a slick presentation, or a moment of emotional pressure, or our own spiritual willpower. It is something more wonderful than any of those things.

Think of Joy, sitting in that car park, reading letters she had never known existed. The reaching had been going on for years before she knew it. But the moment she read them, a response was called for. She could not simply go on as before.

It is like that with us. It is God himself – rich in mercy, moved by love – who has been reaching toward us. He has already spoken. The question is whether you have come to believe it yet – and whether, today, you are living like someone who has come home.
Questions for Further Reflection

elements.question: Has there been a moment when you realised your coming to faith wasn’t down to your own searching or wisdom – that God had been at work before you knew it? What did that feel like?
elements.answerPlaceholder: Your reflections...

elements.question: Think of someone in your life whose heart seems closed to God. God takes the initiative and he can reach anyone – how does that truth change how you pray for them or speak to them?
elements.answerPlaceholder: Your reflections...

elements.question: When you share your faith (or think about doing so), is Christ’s death and what it achieved central – or do you tend to lead with something else (your own story, etc.)? What does that tell you?
elements.answerPlaceholder: Your reflections...

elements.text:
For three years, Tom had known he needed to sort things out with his dad. The falling-out had been his fault; he knew that. He’d rehearsed the conversation a hundred times. Every Christmas, every birthday, he’d feel the weight of it. He genuinely wanted things to be different. But he never made the call.

Feeling sorry wasn’t the problem. He was sorry. He just hadn’t turned around. One night he finally did. He drove to his father’s house, knocked on the door, and said the things he’d been saying only to himself for three years. It was the same words he’d rehearsed. But this time, he was actually there.

elements.question:
• What do you think of when you hear this word?
  • What do you think it really means?
  • Think of your own answer first, then look at ours.
elements.answer:
Repentance is often confused with feeling guilty, or promising to try harder. But you can feel terrible about something and never actually change.


Real repentance is a change of mind and a change of direction. Not just a feeling, but a turning. Regret looks inward. Repentance turns toward God.

elements.passageText: Then he opened their minds so they could understand the scriptures, 46and said to them, ‘Thus it stands written that the Christ would suffer and would rise from the dead on the third day, 47and repentance for the forgiveness of sins would be proclaimed in his name to all nations, beginning from Jerusalem.

elements.question: What is to be proclaimed in Jesus’ name?
elements.answerPlaceholder: Remember to answer the question from the passage
elements.sampleAnswer: Repentance for the forgiveness of sins is to be proclaimed in Jesus’ name to all nations. Jesus ties this proclamation directly to his death and resurrection — it flows from who he is and what he has done. Repentance is not an optional extra but part of the core message to be preached to the whole world.
elements.questionHint: Verse 47 names both what is to be proclaimed and to whom — what is the content of the message, and how wide is its intended reach?

elements.passageText: <sup>30</sup>‘Therefore I will judge each person according to his conduct, O house of Israel, declares the Sovereign LORD. Repent and turn from all your rebellious deeds; they will not be a stumbling block bringing iniquity upon you. <sup>31</sup>Throw away all your sins you have committed and fashion yourselves a new heart and a new spirit! Why should you die, O house of Israel? <sup>32</sup>For I take no pleasure in the death of anyone, declares the Sovereign LORD. Repent and live!

elements.question: Why is repentance urgent?
elements.answerPlaceholder: Remember to answer the question from the passage
elements.sampleAnswer: Repentance is urgent because the alternative is death — God will judge each person according to their conduct, and unrepented sin leads to destruction. The question ’Why should you die?’ makes the stakes explicit: the choice is between repentance and death. Yet the urgency is not rooted in fear alone — God declares he takes no pleasure in the death of anyone. The call is ’Repent and live!’ — life is on offer, but not indefinitely.
elements.questionHint: God asks a pointed question in verse 31 — ‘Why should you die?’ What does this question reveal about what hangs in the balance, and what does verse 32 tell you about God’s attitude toward those who face that outcome?

elements.text: Repentance is more than regret. We’ve all felt bad without changing. Real repentance is a turning – a change of direction, not just of feeling. It is a decisive turning from sin toward God. It’s not optional, and it’s not reserved for people who’ve done especially terrible things. It is God’s call to every one of us. It starts on the inside – a genuine change of heart and mind – but it never stays there.

elements.passageText: <sup>9</sup>Now I rejoice — not because you were grieved, but because you were grieved to repentance. For you were grieved in a godly way, so that you were not harmed by us in any way. <sup>10</sup>For sadness as intended by God produces a repentance that leads to salvation, leaving no regret, but worldly sadness brings about death.

elements.question: What two kinds of grief does Paul distinguish?
elements.answerPlaceholder: Remember to answer the question from the passage
elements.sampleAnswer: Paul distinguishes between godly grief and worldly grief. Godly grief — sorrow oriented toward God — produces repentance that leads to salvation, and leaves no regret. Worldly grief — sorrow oriented toward oneself, one’s reputation, or one’s consequences — brings about death. Both involve genuine emotional pain, but they move in entirely different directions.
elements.questionHint: Paul names two types of grief and traces where each one leads — what are the two different outcomes he says each kind of grief produces?

elements.question: How can we tell the difference?
elements.answerPlaceholder: Remember to answer the question from the passage
elements.sampleAnswer: The difference shows in where the grief leads. Godly grief leads to repentance and then to salvation, and leaves no regret — it produces a genuine turning. Worldly grief stays focused on the self (shame, consequences, damaged reputation) and results in death rather than transformation. The test is not how intense the feeling is but what it produces: does this sorrow move me toward God and change, or does it spiral inward?
elements.questionHint: Paul says godly grief leaves ’no regret’ after it has done its work — what does the presence or absence of that regret suggest about which direction each kind of grief is ultimately focused?

elements.passageText: <sup>16</sup>And see to it that no one becomes an immoral or godless person like Esau, who sold his own birthright for a single meal. <sup>17</sup>For you know that later when he wanted to inherit the blessing, he was rejected, for he found no opportunity for repentance, although he sought the blessing with tears.

elements.question:
• What do you think of when you hear this word?
  • What do you think it really means?
  • Think of your own answer first, then look at ours.
elements.answer:
Faith is often treated as a feeling (‘I just need to believe more strongly’) or vague spiritual optimism. But biblical faith isn’t about the intensity of your trust – it’s about the object of it.


Weak faith in a reliable person is better than strong confidence in the wrong one. Faith means entrusting yourself to a specific person – Jesus – and what he has specifically done. Faith means depending on him.

elements.question: What did Esau seek? What could he not find?
elements.answerPlaceholder: Remember to answer the question from the passage
elements.sampleAnswer: Esau sought the blessing — the inheritance he had previously despised and sold for a single meal. But he found no opportunity for repentance. He wept and sought the blessing with tears, but the tears were not repentance. He wanted the consequence reversed, not the sin dealt with. The moment had passed, and no amount of emotional distress could recover what his godlessness had forfeited.
elements.questionHint: Notice that Esau came seeking the blessing with tears — but what had he actually sold, and what does the fact that he couldn’t find ’repentance’ suggest he was really after?

elements.text:
You can feel sorrow without turning to God. Tears are not the same as repentance. Repentance isn’t self-hatred. It’s not a promise to try harder. It is turning from sin because we see it for what it is – rebellion against a holy and gracious God.

Repentance and faith are two sides of the same coin. Repentance is letting go – loosening your grip on sin and self-sufficiency. Faith is reaching out – trusting your weight to Christ. They belong together; you can’t genuinely do one without the other. Faith isn’t a feeling, or a vague hope. It is trust in a specific person – Jesus – and in what he has specifically done. On days when that trust feels weak, what matters is not the strength of our grip. It is what we are holding on to, and who is holding on to us.

elements.passageText: <sup>16</sup>For this is the way God loved the world: He gave his one and only Son, so that everyone who believes in him will not perish but have eternal life. <sup>17</sup>For God did not send his Son into the world to condemn the world, but that the world should be saved through him. <sup>18</sup>The one who believes in him is not condemned. The one who does not believe has been condemned already, because he has not believed in the name of the one and only Son of God.

elements.question: What is promised to whoever believes? And what if we don’t?
elements.answerPlaceholder: Remember to answer the question from the passage
elements.sampleAnswer: To whoever believes, Jesus promises eternal life and freedom from condemnation — the one who believes will not perish and is not condemned. For those who do not believe, the passage is stark: they have been condemned already, because they have not believed in the name of God’s only Son. Unbelief is not a neutral position; it is a state of existing condemnation.
elements.questionHint: The passage gives two contrasting outcomes — one for the believer and one for the unbeliever. Notice that Jesus says the unbeliever ’has been condemned already’, not ’will be condemned’ — what does that present tense tell you about the seriousness of unbelief?

elements.passageText: <sup>7</sup>But these assets I have come to regard as liabilities because of Christ. <sup>8</sup>More than that, I now regard all things as liabilities compared to the far greater value of knowing Christ Jesus my Lord, for whom I have suffered the loss of all things — indeed, I regard them as dung!

elements.question: What does Paul give up?
elements.answerPlaceholder: Remember to answer the question from the passage
elements.sampleAnswer: Paul gives up everything he once counted as gain — his religious pedigree, his law-keeping, his status, and indeed all things. He has suffered the loss of all things and regards them as dung. This is not reluctant sacrifice but a willing revaluation: what he once prized he now sees as worthless compared to knowing Christ.
elements.questionHint: Paul uses an escalating structure — in verse 7 he calls his former assets ’liabilities’, but then in verse 8 he goes even further. What word does he use in verse 8, and what does that escalation tell you about how complete his renunciation is?

elements.passageText: More than that, I now regard all things as liabilities compared to the far greater value of knowing Christ Jesus my Lord, for whom I have suffered the loss of all things — indeed, I regard them as dung! — that I may gain Christ, 9and be found in him, not because I have my own righteousness derived from the law, but because I have the righteousness that comes by way of Christ’s faithfulness — a righteousness from God that is in fact based on Christ’s faithfulness.

elements.question:
• What do you think of when you hear these words?
  • What do you think they really means?
  • Think of your own answer first, then look at ours.
elements.answer:
’Justified’ and ’righteous’ are sometimes read as meaning ’good enough’ or ’morally improved’ – or even as meaning ’self-righteous.’ But in Scripture, to be justified means to be declared right with God – declared innocent – not because you’ve become righteous, but because Christ’s righteousness has been credited to you.


It’s a legal verdict, not a character assessment. God doesn’t declare you righteous because you are. He makes you righteous by declaring it.

elements.question: What does he desire instead?
elements.answerPlaceholder: Remember to answer the question from the passage
elements.sampleAnswer: Paul desires to gain Christ and be found in him — and specifically to possess the righteousness that comes through Christ’s faithfulness, a righteousness from God, rather than any righteousness of his own derived from the law. He wants to be located in Christ, not in his own religious achievements. His goal is not a better version of himself but union with Christ.
elements.questionHint: Paul describes wanting two things in these verses: to ’gain’ something and to ’be found’ somewhere — what are those two things, and how does the righteousness he wants in verse 9 differ from the one he has given up?

elements.text: Faith is not just agreeing that facts are true. It is entrusting ourselves to Christ alone.

elements.passageText: <sup>8</sup>For by grace you are saved through faith, and this is not from yourselves, it is the gift of God; <sup>9</sup>it is not from works, so that no one can boast.

elements.question: By what are we saved? And through what?
elements.answerPlaceholder: Remember to answer the question from the passage
elements.sampleAnswer: We are saved by grace — that is the cause and the basis. And it comes to us through faith — that is the means by which we receive it. Paul is careful to say that even this (the whole package of grace received through faith) is not from ourselves but is the gift of God.
elements.questionHint: Paul uses two different prepositions — ‘by’ and ’through’ — to describe grace and faith respectively. What distinction do those two words draw between the role grace plays and the role faith plays in salvation?

elements.question: What role do works play? And what does this do to boasting?
elements.answerPlaceholder: Remember to answer the question from the passage
elements.sampleAnswer: Works play no role in our salvation — the passage explicitly rules them out. And the reason Paul gives is precisely to eliminate boasting: if salvation were partly based on our works, we would have grounds to take some credit for it. But because it is entirely of grace, received through faith, and is God’s gift, there is nothing left to boast about. The design of salvation rules out human pride at the level of foundation.
elements.questionHint: Verse 9 says salvation is ’not from works’ and then immediately gives a reason — what is that reason, and what does it tell you about the connection between how we are saved and whether we can take any credit for it?

elements.passageText: <sup>4</sup>Now to the one who works, his pay is not credited due to grace but due to obligation. <sup>5</sup>But to the one who does not work, but believes in the one who declares the ungodly righteous, his faith is credited as righteousness.

elements.question: What is the difference between wages and a gift?
elements.answerPlaceholder: Remember to answer the question from the passage
elements.sampleAnswer: Wages are earned — they are credited due to obligation, because the worker has a right to what they have worked for. A gift is given freely, due to the giver’s grace rather than the recipient’s merit. Paul’s point is that if we could earn righteousness by working, God would owe it to us — but that is not how salvation operates. It is received as a gift by the one who believes, not earned by the one who works.
elements.questionHint: Paul says wages are credited ’due to obligation’ — think about what obligation means in a work relationship. Why would it be a problem if God’s righteousness worked the same way?

elements.question: How are the ungodly justified?
elements.answerPlaceholder: Remember to answer the question from the passage
elements.sampleAnswer: The ungodly are justified by believing in the one who declares the ungodly righteous — that is, by faith, not by works. Their faith is credited to them as righteousness. Strikingly, Paul describes the recipient as ’ungodly’ — it is not the almost-righteous who are justified by faith, but the ungodly. God justifies precisely those who have no righteousness of their own to offer.
elements.questionHint: Paul’s description of who God justifies in verse 5 is deliberately striking — he doesn’t say God justifies the ’almost good enough’. What word does he use, and why is that word important?

elements.text: Faith does not earn salvation. It receives it.

elements.passageText: <sup>14</sup>Now after John was imprisoned, Jesus went into Galilee and proclaimed the gospel of God. <sup>15</sup>He said, ‘The time is fulfilled and the kingdom of God is near. Repent and believe the gospel!’

elements.question: What two responses does Jesus command?
elements.answerPlaceholder: Remember to answer the question from the passage
elements.sampleAnswer: Jesus commands repentance and belief — ‘Repent and believe the gospel.’ These are both commands, not suggestions, and they are given together as the proper response to the arrival of the kingdom of God. The announcement (‘the time is fulfilled and the kingdom of God is near’) is what makes both responses urgent.
elements.questionHint: Jesus issues his call in response to an announcement he has just made — what is that announcement, and how does it explain why both repentance and belief are commanded as an immediate response?

elements.question: Why do these belong together?
elements.answerPlaceholder: Remember to answer the question from the passage
elements.sampleAnswer: Jesus issues them as a single, inseparable command in response to one event — the arrival of the kingdom. Repentance and belief are two sides of the same response: repentance is the turning away from the old life and its allegiances, and belief is the turning toward Christ and what he has done. You cannot genuinely do one without the other. To truly believe the gospel is to turn from what contradicts it, and to genuinely repent is to turn toward the one the gospel announces.
elements.questionHint: Jesus gives these two commands in a single breath, in response to a single announcement — what does the fact that they come together, without separation, suggest about whether they can be treated as independent responses?

elements.passageText: <sup>23</sup>Then he said to them all, ‘If anyone wants to become my follower, he must deny himself, take up his cross daily, and follow me. <sup>24</sup>For whoever wants to save his life will lose it, but whoever loses his life for my sake will save it. <sup>25</sup>For what does it benefit a person if he gains the whole world but loses or forfeits himself?

elements.question: What does Jesus say about following him? What’s at stake?
elements.answerPlaceholder: Remember to answer the question from the passage
elements.sampleAnswer: Jesus says that following him requires three things: self-denial, taking up one’s cross daily, and following him. This is not a one-time act but a daily pattern. What is at stake is life itself — in the deepest sense. Whoever tries to save their life (keeping self at the centre) will lose it; whoever loses their life for Jesus’ sake will save it. Gaining the whole world while losing oneself is presented as the worst possible trade.
elements.questionHint: Jesus describes a paradox in verse 24 — losing your life in order to save it. What does this paradox suggest about what ’life’ actually means in his understanding, and what is truly at stake in the decision to follow him?

elements.text:
Repentance and faith are inseparable. You can’t cling to sin and cling to Christ at the same time. Repentance without faith can spiral into despair – ‘I’ve turned from sin, but there’s nothing to turn to.’ And faith without repentance can become a kind of comfortable excuse – ‘God will forgive me, so it doesn’t really matter how I live.’ True conversion holds both together.

Conversion is not adding Jesus to our existing priorities. It is reordering our lives around him. Repentance is a decisive turning from sin, and faith is personal trust in Christ and his finished work. We don’t earn our salvation by producing them – they are gifts of God’s grace.

Maybe you can look back to a moment when this turn happened for you – a specific day, or a slow dawning you couldn’t pin to a date. Or maybe you are honestly not sure whether it has happened at all. Either way, the invitation standing in front of you right now is the same one it has always been: turn from sin and trust in Christ. Not eventually. Today. That is what conversion is – and it is available to you right now.
Questions for Further Reflection

elements.question: Can you think of a time when you felt genuine sorrow about a sin but didn’t actually turn from it? What was going on? What is the difference between what you felt then and what repentance would have looked like?
elements.answerPlaceholder: Your reflections...

elements.question: Do you ever try to ’make up for’ sin through religious activity rather than returning to Christ in repentance and faith?
elements.answerPlaceholder: Your reflections...

elements.question: If someone who knows you well were asked whether your life shows repentance and faith, what might they say?
elements.answerPlaceholder: Your reflections...

elements.text:
Nobody expected Daniel to change. Least of all Daniel. He’d been in prison three times by his thirties. People who loved him had learned, the hard way, to keep expectations low. His sister had stopped hoping out loud. His mother still prayed, but quietly, mostly to herself.

Then something happened that no one could fully explain – including Daniel. He didn’t clean up his life and then become a Christian. Something shifted in him that he hadn’t chosen and couldn’t have manufactured. Old things started to feel hollow. He found himself wanting what he’d always mocked. His sister noticed first. She said it was like talking to a different person. Same face. Different person.

What had happened? And why then, and not the twenty times before?

elements.question:
• What do you think of when you hear this phrase?
  • What do you think it really means?
  • Think of your own answer first, then look at ours.
elements.answer:
Culturally, ‘born again’ has come to mean a particular type of religious personality – emotional, conservative, or fervent. But Jesus uses it to describe something that happens to a person, not something they work up in themselves.


It’s not a style of Christianity. It’s the entry point for all of it – as transformative as being born the first time.

elements.passageText: <sup>25</sup>I will sprinkle clean water on you and you will be clean from all your impurities. I will purify you from all your idols. <sup>26</sup>I will give you a new heart, and I will put a new spirit within you. I will remove the heart of stone from your body and give you a heart of flesh. <sup>27</sup>I will put my Spirit within you; I will take the initiative and you will obey my statutes and carefully observe my ordinances.

elements.question: What does God promise to do for his people?
elements.answerPlaceholder: Remember to answer the question from the passage
elements.sampleAnswer: God promises to cleanse his people from impurity and idolatry, give them a new heart and a new spirit, remove the heart of stone and replace it with a heart of flesh, and put his own Spirit within them — so that they will obey his statutes. Every promise is something God will do: the initiative, the transformation, and the resulting obedience all flow from his action.
elements.questionHint: Count how many times the phrase ’I will’ appears in these three verses — what does that pattern tell you about who is doing the work of transformation?

elements.question: How much of the initiative is God’s and how much is ours?
elements.answerPlaceholder: Remember to answer the question from the passage
elements.sampleAnswer: The initiative is entirely God’s. Every action in the passage is God’s action: ‘I will sprinkle... I will give... I will remove... I will put.’ Even the obedience that results is said to follow from God taking the initiative (verse 27). The people are the recipients throughout — they receive cleansing, a new heart, a new spirit, and God’s indwelling presence. None of the transformation originates with them.
elements.questionHint: Look at who is the subject of every verb in this passage — who does the acting, and who is acted upon? What does that grammatical pattern suggest about where the initiative for transformation lies?

elements.text: Centuries before Jesus, God had already promised this: not gradual improvement, but transformation from the inside out. This is not self-reformation. It is divine heart surgery.

elements.passageText: <sup>8</sup>The wind blows wherever it will, and you hear the sound it makes, but do not know where it comes from and where it is going. So it is with everyone who is born of the Spirit.

elements.question: How does Jesus describe the work of the Spirit?
elements.answerPlaceholder: Remember to answer the question from the passage
elements.sampleAnswer: Jesus compares the Spirit’s work to the wind: it blows wherever it wills, its sound can be heard but its origin and destination cannot be tracked or controlled. The Spirit works freely and sovereignly — like the wind, his work is real and its effects are observable, but it operates beyond human prediction or control.
elements.questionHint: Jesus uses a comparison — the Spirit’s work is ’like’ the wind. What two things does he say we can and cannot know about the wind, and what does that suggest about what we can and cannot control about new birth?

elements.question: What does this teach us about human control over new birth?
elements.answerPlaceholder: Remember to answer the question from the passage
elements.sampleAnswer: New birth cannot be manufactured, planned, or controlled by human beings.
