Just as no one can command the wind, no one can produce the Spirit’s regenerating work in themselves or in another. We can no more arrange our own new birth than we arranged our first birth. This rules out any technique, programme, or emotional pressure that claims to produce spiritual rebirth on demand. The Spirit’s work is sovereign — he blows where he wills.
elements.questionHint: If the Spirit works like the wind — beyond human prediction or direction — what does that imply about methods or strategies that claim to produce new birth in people?

elements.text: Entry into the kingdom needs new birth. The wind is real - you can see the trees bend, feel it on your face – but you can’t see the wind itself, or control where it blows. The Spirit’s work of new birth is like that. Its effects are visible, but the work is God’s, not ours.

elements.passageText: ‘I tell you the solemn truth, the one who hears my word and believes the one who sent me has eternal life and will not be condemned, but has crossed over from death to life.

elements.question: What does Jesus promise to the one who hears and believes?
elements.answerPlaceholder: Remember to answer the question from the passage
elements.sampleAnswer: Jesus promises three things to the one who hears his word and believes: eternal life, freedom from condemnation, and a completed crossing from death to life. Notably these are all stated in the present tense — the believer ’has’ eternal life and ’has crossed over’ already. This is not merely a future hope but a present reality for those who believe.
elements.questionHint: Notice the tenses Jesus uses — he says the believer ’has’ eternal life and ’has crossed over’, not ’will have’ or ’will cross over’. What does the present tense tell you about when these promises take effect?

elements.passageText: <sup>1</sup>Everyone who believes that Jesus is the Christ has been fathered by God, and everyone who loves the father loves the child fathered by him.

elements.question: What does believing that Jesus is the Christ show about us?
elements.answerPlaceholder: Remember to answer the question from the passage
elements.sampleAnswer: Believing that Jesus is the Christ shows that we have been fathered by God — that is, born of God. The verb ’has been fathered’ is in the past tense, indicating that new birth has already happened. Faith is therefore evidence of prior new birth, not the cause of it. Someone who genuinely believes has already been brought to life by God.
elements.questionHint: The verse says that everyone who believes ’has been fathered by God’ — the new birth is described as already having taken place. What does that sequence (new birth, then faith) suggest about which one causes the other?

elements.question: How does faith being evidence of new birth guard us from pride (‘I was wise enough to believe’) and despair (‘My faith is weak’)?
elements.answerPlaceholder: Remember to answer the question from the passage
elements.sampleAnswer: If faith is evidence that God has already fathered us, then faith is not our achievement — it is something God produced in us through new birth. This eliminates pride: we did not believe because we were wise or spiritually perceptive; we believed because God gave us new birth. And it eliminates despair: if even weak faith is evidence of new birth, then the question is not ’Is my faith strong enough?’ but ’Has God brought me to life?’ — and a faint but genuine trust in Christ says he has.
elements.questionHint: If believing is the result of being ’fathered by God’ rather than the cause of it, what happens to the claim ’I believed because I was wise enough to respond’ — and what happens to the fear ’My faith is too weak to count’?

elements.text: New birth is life from the dead. If you find yourself trusting in Christ – even weakly, even with doubts – that trust shows something has already happened in you. You didn’t generate it from nowhere. God gave it. Faith is the fruit of new birth, not the root of it.

elements.passageText: <sup>1</sup>The hand of the LORD was on me, and he brought me out by the Spirit of the LORD and set me down in the middle of the valley; it was full of bones. <sup>2</sup>He made me walk all around among them. I realized there were a great many bones in the valley and they were very dry. <sup>3</sup>He said to me, ‘Son of man, can these bones live?’ I said to him, ‘Sovereign LORD, you know.’ <sup>4</sup>Then he said to me, ‘Prophesy over these bones, and tell them: ‘Dry bones, hear the word of the LORD. <sup>5</sup>This is what the Sovereign LORD says to these bones: Look, I am about to infuse breath into you and you will live. <sup>6</sup>I will put tendons on you and muscles over you and cover you with skin; I will put breath in you and you will live. Then you will know that I am the LORD.’’ <sup>7</sup>So I prophesied as I was commanded. There was a sound when I prophesied — I heard a rattling, and the bones came together, bone to bone. <sup>8</sup>As I watched, I saw tendons on them, then muscles appeared, and skin covered over them from above, but there was no breath in them. <sup>9</sup>He said to me, ‘Prophesy to the breath, — prophesy, son of man — and tell the breath: ‘This is what the Sovereign LORD says: Come from the four winds, O breath, and breathe on these corpses so that they will live.’’ <sup>10</sup>So I prophesied as I was commanded, and the breath came into them; they lived and stood on their feet, an extremely large army.

elements.question: What question does God ask, and why is it significant?
elements.answerPlaceholder: Remember to answer the question from the passage
elements.sampleAnswer: God asks: ‘Can these bones live?’ It is significant because the bones are described as very dry — as dead as it is possible to be, with no natural hope of revival. Ezekiel’s answer (‘Sovereign LORD, you know’) is itself significant: he does not say yes or no, but deflects the question back to God — acknowledging that whether dead things can live is entirely a question about God’s power, not the bones’ potential.
elements.questionHint: Ezekiel does not answer ’yes’ or ’no’ to God’s question — he says ’Sovereign LORD, you know.’ What does that response tell you about where the power to answer the question actually lies?

elements.question: What does this teach us about the source of spiritual life?
elements.answerPlaceholder: Remember to answer the question from the passage
elements.sampleAnswer: The vision teaches that spiritual life comes entirely from God — it cannot emerge from what is already dead. The bones do nothing; the breath comes from God, commanded by his word through his prophet. Life is infused from outside, not generated from within. This is a picture of regeneration: God speaking life into the spiritually dead, not the dead improving themselves into life.
elements.questionHint: The bones in the vision cannot do anything to bring themselves to life — so what are the two instruments God uses to bring life to them, and what does that tell you about where spiritual life originates?

elements.passageText: <sup>6</sup>For God, who said ‘Let light shine out of darkness,’ is the one who shined in our hearts to give us the light of the glorious knowledge of God in the face of Christ.

elements.question: What comparison does Paul make with creation?
elements.answerPlaceholder: Remember to answer the question from the passage
elements.sampleAnswer: Paul compares the new birth to the original act of creation when God said ’Let light shine out of darkness.’ Just as God spoke light into the primeval darkness — out of nothing, by his word alone — so he shines into dark human hearts to bring the knowledge of God. Regeneration is a creative act of the same magnitude and character as the original creation.
elements.questionHint: Paul connects what God does in the human heart to something God did ’in the beginning’ — what was that first act, and why is that particular comparison significant for understanding how new birth happens?

elements.question: How is this ’shining’ more than just learning new information?
elements.answerPlaceholder: Remember to answer the question from the passage
elements.sampleAnswer: When God originally said ’Let there be light’, light did not previously exist — his word brought it into being. In the same way, Paul says God shines ’in our hearts’ — not merely presenting information to existing spiritual faculties, but creating the capacity to receive the knowledge of God where none existed before. Learning new information works on what is already there; this shining creates something new. It is a creative act, not an educational one.
elements.questionHint: At creation, darkness did not ’learn’ to become light — God created light where there was none. What does that creative parallel suggest about what God is doing in the heart, compared to simply giving us new facts to consider?

elements.text: Regeneration is not just improvement – it is creation. The God who said ’Let there be light’ must also shine into dark hearts.

elements.passageText: <sup>14</sup>For the love of Christ controls us, since we have concluded this, that Christ died for all; therefore all have died. <sup>15</sup>And he died for all so that those who live should no longer live for themselves but for him who died for them and was raised.

elements.question: What change of direction does new birth bring? And why?
elements.answerPlaceholder: Remember to answer the question from the passage
elements.sampleAnswer: New birth brings a fundamental reorientation away from living for oneself and toward living for Christ. The reason given is the cross: Christ died for all so that those who live should no longer live for themselves but for him. The death of Christ changes the entire basis of life. The driving force for this change is not duty or willpower but the love of Christ, which ’controls’ those who have grasped what he has done.
elements.questionHint: Verse 15 gives a specific reason why those who live should no longer live for themselves — what is that reason, and how does the death and resurrection of Christ logically produce a change in who we live for?

elements.question: What does this mean for the ’engine’ of Christian obedience?
elements.answerPlaceholder: Remember to answer the question from the passage
elements.sampleAnswer: Paul says it is the love of Christ that ’controls’ us — that word suggests a compelling, motivating force that drives behaviour from within. This means the engine of Christian obedience is not duty, fear, or self-discipline, but an understanding of and response to Christ’s love shown at the cross. Obedience powered by grasping what Christ has done is fundamentally different from obedience as mere rule-keeping.
elements.questionHint: Paul says the love of Christ ’controls’ those who have grasped what Christ did — what does the word ’controls’ suggest about where the motivation for Christian living comes from, and how that differs from simply trying harder to be good?

elements.passageText: <sup>7</sup>Dear friends, let us love one another, because love is from God, and everyone who loves has been fathered by God and knows God.

elements.question: What does love reveal about someone’s spiritual birth?
elements.answerPlaceholder: Remember to answer the question from the passage
elements.sampleAnswer: Love reveals that someone has been fathered by God and knows God. Because love originates from God, its presence in a person is evidence that God has worked in them — it is a sign of new birth. Love is not just a nice trait; it is diagnostic of spiritual life. Its presence points to God as the source.
elements.questionHint: John gives a reason for the command to love: ‘because love is from God.’ What does that reasoning tell you about the relationship between being born of God and having the capacity to love in this way?

elements.passageText: <sup>8</sup>The person who does not love does not know God, because God is love. <sup>9</sup>By this the love of God is revealed in us: that God has sent his one and only Son into the world so that we may live through him. <sup>10</sup>In this is love: not that we have loved God, but that he loved us and sent his Son to be the atoning sacrifice for our sins. <sup>11</sup>Dear friends, if God so loved us, we also ought to love one another. <sup>12</sup>No one has seen God at any time. If we love one another, God resides in us, and his love is perfected in us.

elements.question: Why is love evidence of new life?
elements.answerPlaceholder: Remember to answer the question from the passage
elements.sampleAnswer: Love is evidence of new life because God is love — love is not merely something God does but what he is. Therefore those who genuinely love share in something of God’s own nature, which only happens through new birth. Verse 8 goes further: the person who does not love does not know God. Love is not an add-on to knowing God; it is inseparable from it. The love shown among believers is the visible expression of God’s own love taking up residence in those who are his.
elements.questionHint: John makes a striking claim in verse 8: not just that God loves, but that ’God is love.’ What does that identification — love as God’s very nature — tell you about why love in a person is evidence that God is at work in them?

elements.text: Regeneration changes what we love. The new birth produces a new love for God and for his people, a new sensitivity to sin, and a new hunger for righteousness. Not perfectly – but genuinely.

elements.passageText: <sup>22</sup>You have purified your souls by obeying the truth in order to show sincere mutual love. So love one another earnestly from a pure heart. <sup>23</sup>You have been born anew, not from perishable but from imperishable seed, through the living and enduring word of God.

elements.question: What are believers said to be born through?
elements.answerPlaceholder: Remember to answer the question from the passage
elements.sampleAnswer: Believers are said to have been born anew through the living and enduring word of God — not from perishable seed but from imperishable seed. The instrument of new birth is the word of God: the gospel message, which is alive, enduring, and of a completely different and permanent order compared to anything human.
elements.questionHint: Peter describes the seed through which believers are born as ’imperishable’ and ’living and enduring’ — what do those qualities suggest about why the word of God is a fitting instrument for producing a new birth that lasts?

elements.question: So what is the relationship between the Spirit and the gospel?
elements.answerPlaceholder: Remember to answer the question from the passage
elements.sampleAnswer: The passage shows that new birth comes through the word of God — the gospel. Combined with what we have seen elsewhere (John 3:8, the Spirit blowing like wind; James 1:18, birth through the message of truth), the picture is that the Spirit works through the word. The Spirit is the agent; the gospel is the instrument he uses to bring about new birth. This means that preaching the gospel is not merely human activity — it is the means the Spirit uses to create new life.
elements.questionHint: Peter says new birth comes ’through the living and enduring word of God’ — given what we have seen elsewhere about the Spirit being the one who brings new birth, what does that suggest about how the Spirit and the word work together?

elements.text:
God’s Spirit works through God’s word. Regeneration isn’t emotional intensity, a signed card, church attendance, or following the right rules. It’s an inward transformation that works its way out.

New birth is God’s promise and God’s work. We don’t produce it, any more than a newborn chooses to be born. But it shows up: a hunger for God that wasn’t there before; a sensitivity to sin so that you want to confess rather than hide; a love for other believers that surprises you. Not perfectly, and not all at once – but genuinely.

If you find those things stirring in you, even faintly, that is not something you generated. God is at work. And if you are thinking right now of someone whose heart seems completely closed to God – hold onto this: so did yours, once. No one is beyond the reach of the Spirit who brings the dead to life. Pray for them. Keep speaking. The wind blows where it will.
Questions for Further Reflection

elements.question: Does knowing that regeneration is God’s work give you more confidence or less? Are there ways in which you have treated your spiritual life as something you have to maintain by effort?
elements.answerPlaceholder: Your reflections...

elements.question: Have you ever had a period of outward religious activity (going to church, reading your Bible, behaving well) without much genuine inner life? What was that like? What was missing?
elements.answerPlaceholder: Your reflections...

elements.question: Where have you seen evidence of God’s transforming work in your own life – in your desires, relationships, or response to sin? Is there an area where you are still hoping for that change?
elements.answerPlaceholder: Your reflections...

elements.text:
Marcus became a Christian in his twenties, largely through reading the Bible on his own. For the first few months, he kept it private. He prayed alone, read alone, and told no one. He thought of his faith as something between him and God.

Then a friend invited him to a small group. He resisted for months – but eventually went. He was surprised to find that he needed them, and they seemed to need him too. He hadn’t expected that.

Many people think of conversion as purely personal – a ’personal relationship’ between them and God – and this is genuinely true. But in the New Testament, those who come to Christ are not left spiritually alone. When God gives someone a new heart, he also places them in a new family – among brothers and sisters who belong to the same Father.

elements.passageText: <sup>41</sup>So those who accepted his message were baptized, and that day about three thousand people were added. <sup>42</sup>They were devoting themselves to the apostles’ teaching and to fellowship, to the breaking of bread and to prayer.

elements.question: What marked the shared life of those who received the word?
elements.answerPlaceholder: Remember to answer the question from the passage
elements.sampleAnswer: The shared life of the first converts was marked by four things they devoted themselves to: the apostles’ teaching, fellowship, the breaking of bread, and prayer. These were not occasional activities but the consistent, committed pattern of their common life together. Notably, they were also baptized — visibly identifying with Christ and with one another.
elements.questionHint: Verse 42 uses the word ’devoting’ — which is a strong word. What four specific things does it say they were devoted to, and what does the word ’devoting’ suggest about the depth of their commitment to these shared practices?

elements.question: What does this suggest is the normal pattern after conversion?
elements.answerPlaceholder: Remember to answer the question from the passage
elements.sampleAnswer: The normal pattern after conversion is immediate visible belonging — baptism — and then ongoing shared life together: teaching, fellowship, shared meals, and prayer. There is no gap between conversion and community in this account. Those who received the word were added to a body of people, not merely to a set of beliefs. The pattern assumes that new Christians belong to a community of other Christians.
elements.questionHint: Notice that immediately after accepting the message, the converts were ’added’ — added to what, and added to whom? What does that word ’added’ imply about conversion’s natural outcome?

elements.text: In the New Testament, conversion is never merely private. It is always visible. It produces belonging, shared worship, shared life.

elements.passageText: <sup>12</sup>For just as the body is one and yet has many members, and all the members of the body — though many — are one body, so too is Christ. <sup>13</sup>For in one Spirit we were all baptized into one body. Whether Jews or Greeks or slaves or free, we were all made to drink of the one Spirit.

elements.question: What image describes the church? How do people enter it?
elements.answerPlaceholder: Remember to answer the question from the passage
elements.sampleAnswer: The church is described as a body — one body with many members, each belonging to the whole. People enter it by being baptized by the one Spirit into the one body. This is not self-initiated joining: it is the Spirit’s work that incorporates people into the body. The diversity of members (Jews, Greeks, slaves, free) is explicitly noted, yet all are made to drink of the same Spirit.
elements.questionHint: Paul says ’in one Spirit we were all baptized into one body’ — what does the passive voice (‘were baptized’) and the mention of the Spirit suggest about who does the incorporating, and whether church membership is something we arrange for ourselves?

elements.question: What does this passage teach about unity among believers?
elements.answerPlaceholder: Remember to answer the question from the passage
elements.sampleAnswer: Unity among believers is real and given, not merely aspirational. The church is one body — the many members do not merely cooperate but actually belong to each other in a single organism. And this unity crosses all human divisions: Jew and Greek, slave and free are all one in the body through the one Spirit. The basis of unity is not shared background or preference but the common Spirit all have received.
elements.questionHint: Paul specifically mentions groups that would have been sharply divided in the ancient world — Jews and Greeks, slaves and free. What does their inclusion in the same body tell you about the kind of unity the Spirit creates?

elements.text: The church is not a voluntary association of like-minded individuals. It is a body formed by Christ. To be joined to Christ is to be joined to his body. There is no category in the New Testament for a Christian without a church.

elements.passageText: <sup>4</sup>So as you come to him, a living stone rejected by men but chosen and precious in God’s sight, <sup>5</sup>you yourselves, as living stones, are built up as a spiritual house to be a holy priesthood and to offer spiritual sacrifices that are acceptable to God through Jesus Christ.

elements.question: How are believers described? What are they being built into?
elements.answerPlaceholder: Remember to answer the question from the passage
elements.sampleAnswer: Believers are described as living stones — animated by the life of Christ the living stone. They are being built up together into a spiritual house, to be a holy priesthood. No individual stone makes a house; they are built together into something. The corporate nature is essential: the priesthood and the house are collective, not individual.
elements.questionHint: A stone on its own is not a house — it only becomes part of a house when it is built together with other stones. What does that image suggest about whether individual Christians can fulfil their purpose in isolation from other believers?

elements.question: What purpose does this structure serve?
elements.answerPlaceholder: Remember to answer the question from the passage
elements.sampleAnswer: The spiritual house serves as a holy priesthood — to offer spiritual sacrifices acceptable to God through Jesus Christ. The church exists not for itself but for God: to be a worshipping, priestly community that approaches God and offers sacrifices of praise, obedience, and service through Christ. The building has a purpose beyond the building itself.
elements.questionHint: Peter says the spiritual house is built in order to be something — a ’holy priesthood’ with a specific function. What does a priesthood do, and how does that function define the purpose of the whole community being built together?

elements.text: Conversion means being made part of what God is building. Christians are not isolated stones, scattered about. They are being built together – into God’s house.

elements.passageText: <sup>14</sup>Do not become partners with those who do not believe, for what partnership is there between righteousness and lawlessness, or what fellowship does light have with darkness? <sup>15</sup>And what agreement does Christ have with Beliar? Or what does a believer share in common with an unbeliever? <sup>16</sup>And what mutual agreement does the temple of God have with idols? For we are the temple of the living God, just as God said, ‘I will live in them and will walk among them, and I will be their God, and they will be my people.’ <sup>17</sup>Therefore ‘come out from their midst, and be separate,’ says the Lord, ‘and touch no unclean thing, and I will welcome you, <sup>18</sup>and I will be a father to you, and you will be my sons and daughters,’ says the All-Powerful Lord. 7:1 Therefore, since we have these promises, dear friends, let us cleanse ourselves from everything that could defile the flesh and the spirit, and thus accomplish holiness out of reverence for God.’

elements.question: What identity does God claim for his people?
elements.answerPlaceholder: Remember to answer the question from the passage
elements.sampleAnswer: God claims his people as the temple of the living God — the place where he lives and walks. He also claims them as his people (‘I will be their God and they will be my people’) and as his sons and daughters (‘I will be a father to you’). These are relational and intimate identities: God’s household, his dwelling, his family.
elements.questionHint: God gives his people at least three distinct identity descriptions in this passage — can you find all three, and what do they have in common about the kind of relationship God says he has with them?

elements.question: What call flows from that identity?
elements.answerPlaceholder: Remember to answer the question from the passage
elements.sampleAnswer: From the identity of being God’s temple, his people, and his sons and daughters flows the call to be holy and separate — not to be yoked with unbelievers in ways that compromise that identity, to come out and be separate, to cleanse themselves from everything that defiles. The holiness is not self-generated rule-keeping but a response to who they are: you are God’s temple, therefore live accordingly.
elements.questionHint: The passage uses the word ’therefore’ twice — in verse 17 and in 7:1. What comes before each ’therefore’, and what does the structure of ’identity first, then call’ suggest about the basis for the holiness God commands?

elements.text: The church is not just gathered; it is set apart. Conversion brings us into a holy community – one defined not by culture, ethnicity, or shared taste, but by belonging to God. That is a stranger and more demanding bond than any of those.

elements.passageText: <sup>24</sup>And let us take thought of how to spur one another on to love and good works, <sup>25</sup>not abandoning our own meetings, as some are in the habit of doing, but encouraging each other, and even more so because you see the day drawing near.

elements.question: What are believers commanded to consider?
elements.answerPlaceholder: Remember to answer the question from the passage
elements.sampleAnswer: Believers are commanded to consider how to spur one another on to love and good works — to think actively and intentionally about how to provoke growth in others. This requires being present with one another, which is why abandoning the meetings is specifically warned against. The command is not passive attendance but active, purposeful mutual encouragement.
elements.questionHint: The command is not simply to ’attend meetings’ but to ’take thought of how to spur one another on’ — what does the phrase ’take thought’ suggest about the level of intentionality required, and why would that require actually meeting together?

elements.question: What dangers are implied if we neglect to meet together?
elements.answerPlaceholder: Remember to answer the question from the passage
elements.sampleAnswer: The passage implies two dangers from neglecting to meet: others lose the spurring and encouragement they need to persevere in love and good works, and we ourselves lose the same. The urgency is heightened by the phrase ’even more so because you see the day drawing near’ — the approaching judgment makes mutual encouragement more necessary, not less. Isolation at this stage is presented as spiritually dangerous.
elements.questionHint: The writer says we should meet together ’even more so’ as the day draws near — what does the urgency implied by that phrase suggest about what is actually at stake when believers stop encouraging one another?

elements.text: The church’s life together includes mutual responsibility. Love in the church is not sentimental. It is committed, corrective, and persevering.

elements.passageText: <sup>15</sup>‘If your brother sins, go and show him his fault when the two of you are alone. If he listens to you, you have regained your brother. <sup>16</sup>But if he does not listen, take one or two others with you, so that at the testimony of two or three witnesses every matter may be established. <sup>17</sup>If he refuses to listen to them, tell it to the church. If he refuses to listen to the church, treat him like a Gentile or a tax collector.

elements.question: What steps are outlined when a brother sins?
elements.answerPlaceholder: Remember to answer the question from the passage
elements.sampleAnswer: Three escalating steps are outlined: first, go alone to the person and show them their fault privately — if they listen, the matter is resolved; second, if they do not listen, take one or two witnesses; third, if they still refuse, bring it to the church. If they refuse even the church, they are to be treated as an outsider. The process moves from private to small group to congregation, with the goal at each stage being repentance and restoration.
elements.questionHint: Jesus outlines a process that moves through three distinct stages — what is the goal of each stage (look especially at verse 15’s phrase ’you have regained your brother’), and what does that goal reveal about the purpose of the whole process?

elements.question: What does this passage assume about visible belonging?
elements.answerPlaceholder: Remember to answer the question from the passage
elements.sampleAnswer: The passage assumes that believers have a recognised, accountable belonging to a specific community — ‘the church’ that can be told something, that can make a ruling, and to which an unrepentant person’s status can be changed. This only makes sense if church membership is visible and real, not vague or optional. Without identifiable belonging, the final step of bringing the matter to the church would be meaningless.
elements.questionHint: The final step in the process involves telling something to ’the church’ as a body with authority — what does the fact that such a step is even possible assume about the nature of someone’s relationship to that church community?

elements.passageText: <sup>1</sup>So I exhort the elders among you as a fellow elder and a witness of Christ’s sufferings and as one who shares in the glory that will be revealed: <sup>2</sup>Give a shepherd’s care to God’s flock among you, exercising oversight not merely as a duty but willingly under God’s direction, not for shameful profit but eagerly. <sup>3</sup>And do not lord it over those entrusted to you, but be examples to the flock.

elements.question: How are elders to shepherd? What motivations are ruled out?
elements.answerPlaceholder: Remember to answer the question from the passage
elements.sampleAnswer: Elders are to shepherd willingly (not as a grudging duty), eagerly (not for financial gain), and as examples rather than as lords over the flock. Three wrong motivations are explicitly ruled out: compulsion (doing it merely as an obligation), shameful profit (doing it for money), and domineering (using the position for power over people). The model of leadership is servant and example, not ruler.
elements.questionHint: Peter gives three contrasting pairs in verses 2–3 — ‘not this, but that’. What are the three wrong motivations he rules out, and what positive motivation or manner replaces each one?

elements.text:
Christ gives leaders to shepherd his people. Church membership is not merely organisational. It is relational and pastoral. Leaders care for souls. Members are not customers. Together, imperfectly, they are meant to look like Christ.

God does not save us and then leave us alone. He places us in a family – with all the mess, the closeness, the obligation, and the joy that families involve. The church is not a club you join when it suits you, or a service you attend when you feel like it. It is your family. You belong to these people, and they belong to you.

That may feel uncomfortable if church has been disappointing, or if belonging has felt costly. It often is costly. But the alternative – a private Christianity, lived in isolation from the body – is not on offer in the New Testament. You were not saved to be a scattered stone. You were saved to be built into something, together with others, for God’s purposes in the world.

So: where do you belong? Not just where do you attend – but where are you truly known, and truly committed? If you don’t have an answer to that yet, this chapter is an invitation to find one. Conversion is personal, but it is never private: God saves us to be a people.
Questions for Further Reflection

elements.question: Have you ever treated church involvement as optional: attending when convenient, keeping yourself at a certain distance? What were your reasons? How do you feel about those reasons now?
elements.answerPlaceholder: Your reflections...

elements.question: In your own experience, are you more of an attender or a belonger? What would it look like – practically, this week – to move further toward genuine belonging?
elements.answerPlaceholder: Your reflections...

elements.question: Is there a person in your church community you have been avoiding, or a level of commitment you have been resisting? What would it mean to take seriously that this person – and this community – is your family?
elements.answerPlaceholder: Your reflections...

elements.text:
Imagine two trees standing side by side. From a distance they look the same: same height, same leaves, same shape. But when harvest comes, one tree is filled with fruit and the other has none. What looked similar on the outside had different things going on inside.

Sofia had been in church her whole life. She knew the right words, attended every Sunday, and was well liked. But privately, nothing had changed. Her temper still flared at home; she still harboured bitterness she’d never confessed; she still lived as if God’s opinion of her choices didn’t really matter. She wasn’t sure whether she had ever truly come to Christ, or whether she had just become good at looking like she had.

Genuine faith eventually produces visible fruit. That doesn’t mean Christians are perfect – but conversion doesn’t leave a life unchanged.

elements.question:
• What do you think of when you hear this word?
  • What do you think it really means?
  • Think of your own answer first, then look at ours.
elements.answer:
The word ’sanctification’ rarely appears in conversation, but the idea is straightforward: becoming more holy over time. The misunderstanding is treating it as a self-improvement project – trying harder, behaving better, managing sin.


But sanctification is more like a plant growing than a machine being upgraded. The life is already there. The task is staying connected to the source, not generating growth through willpower.

elements.passageText: <sup>16</sup>But I say, live by the Spirit and you will not carry out the desires of the flesh. <sup>17</sup>For the flesh has desires that are opposed to the Spirit, and the Spirit has desires that are opposed to the flesh, for these are in opposition to each other, so that you cannot do what you want.

elements.question: What conflict does Paul describe in these verses?
elements.answerPlaceholder: Remember to answer the question from the passage
elements.sampleAnswer: Paul describes an ongoing internal conflict between the flesh and the Spirit. The flesh has desires opposed to the Spirit, and the Spirit has desires opposed to the flesh — they are fundamentally at war with each other. This conflict is so real that it prevents believers from simply doing whatever they want, because their desires are being pulled in two opposing directions at once.
elements.questionHint: Paul says the flesh and the Spirit each have desires — and that these desires are ’opposed’ to each other. What does the word ’opposed’ tell you about the nature of this conflict, and who are the two parties in it?

elements.question: Why do believers still experience this struggle?
elements.answerPlaceholder: Remember to answer the question from the passage
elements.sampleAnswer: Believers still experience this struggle because the flesh has not been eliminated at conversion — it still exists and still generates desires opposed to the Spirit. The Spirit is now present and active, but the flesh remains. The result is genuine internal conflict: the two are ’in opposition to each other’, so believers feel the pull of both. This is not evidence of false conversion but of genuine life in the Spirit alongside remaining sinful desires.
elements.questionHint: Paul says the flesh and Spirit are both present and both active in the believer — if the flesh were gone at conversion, would this conflict exist? What does its continued existence tell you about the nature of the Christian life this side of glory?

elements.passageText: <sup>22</sup>But the fruit of the Spirit is love, joy, peace, patience, kindness, goodness, faithfulness, <sup>23</sup>gentleness, and self-control. Against such things there is no law.

elements.question: What qualities are described as the fruit of the Spirit?
elements.answerPlaceholder: Remember to answer the question from the passage
elements.sampleAnswer: The fruit of the Spirit is: love, joy, peace, patience, kindness, goodness, faithfulness, gentleness, and self-control. Paul presents these as a singular ’fruit’ — a cluster of qualities that grow together as the Spirit works in the believer’s life. They are not produced by human willpower but by the Spirit’s presence.
elements.questionHint: Notice that Paul uses the singular ’fruit’ rather than ’fruits’ — what might that suggest about whether these nine qualities can be separated from each other, or whether they grow together as a whole?

elements.question: Which of these qualities most challenge or encourage you now?
elements.answerPlaceholder: Remember to answer the question from the passage
elements.sampleAnswer: This is a personal reflection question — there is no single right answer. Consider honestly which quality you most lack, and which you most long for. Some may find patience most challenging in daily relationships; others may struggle most with joy in difficult seasons, or self-control in specific areas. The point is not to feel guilty but to identify where the Spirit’s work is most needed and most welcomed.
elements.questionHint: Look through the list slowly — which quality, if you are honest, do the people closest to you see least of? And which gives you the most hope when you think about the Spirit producing it in you over time?

elements.text: The fruit of the Spirit is not achieved. It is grown. The Spirit is already at work in you – reshaping what you want, what you reach for, what you love.

elements.passageText: <sup>5</sup>Now this is the gospel message we have heard from him and announce to you: God is light, and in him there is no darkness at all. <sup>6</sup>If we say we have fellowship with him and yet keep on walking in the darkness, we are lying and not practicing the truth. <sup>7</sup>But if we walk in the light as he himself is in the light, we have fellowship with one another and the blood of Jesus his Son cleanses us from all sin.

elements.question: What does John say about God’s nature?
elements.answerPlaceholder: Remember to answer the question from the passage
elements.sampleAnswer: John says that God is light, and in him there is no darkness at all. This is an absolute statement: God’s nature is entirely, without qualification, light. There is no mixture, no shadow, no moral ambiguity in him. This foundational claim about God’s character sets up everything that follows about how we should live in relationship with him.
elements.questionHint: John does not just say God has light in him or that he is mostly light — he says ’in him there is no darkness at all.’ What does the absoluteness of that claim imply for anyone who claims to have fellowship with him?

elements.question: What does it mean to ’walk in the light’?
elements.answerPlaceholder: Remember to answer the question from the passage
elements.sampleAnswer: Walking in the light means living openly and honestly before God — the opposite of the person in verse 6 who ’keeps on walking in the darkness’ while claiming fellowship with God. It involves bringing sin into the open rather than concealing it, which is why verse 7 connects walking in the light with the blood of Jesus cleansing from sin. To walk in the light is not to be sinless but to be honest: living without the pretence that sin doesn’t exist or doesn’t matter.
elements.questionHint: John contrasts ’walking in the light’ with ’walking in darkness’ — and the darkness-walker is described as someone who makes a claim about fellowship while living contrary to it. What does that contrast suggest about what walking in the light actually requires of us?

elements.question: Why is honesty about sin an important mark of genuine faith?
elements.answerPlaceholder: Remember to answer the question from the passage
elements.sampleAnswer: Because God is light with no darkness in him, claiming fellowship with him while concealing or excusing sin is self-contradictory — John calls it lying. Genuine faith involves walking in the light: bringing sin into the open before God, which is exactly where the blood of Jesus cleanses. Honesty about sin is not a sign of spiritual failure but of spiritual health — it shows we are in contact with the God who is light, not hiding from him.
elements.questionHint: John says the person who claims fellowship with God while walking in darkness ’is lying and not practicing the truth’ — what does using the word ’lying’ suggest about the relationship between concealing sin and genuine relationship with a God who is entirely light?

elements.text: A converted person doesn’t pretend to be sinless. But they increasingly refuse to live in darkness.

elements.passageText: <sup>9</sup>Do not lie to one another since you have put off the old man with its practices <sup>10</sup>and have been clothed with the new man that is being renewed in knowledge according to the image of the one who created it.

elements.question: What have believers ’put off’ and ’put on’?
elements.answerPlaceholder: Remember to answer the question from the passage
elements.sampleAnswer: Believers have put off the old man — the old self with its practices — and have been clothed with the new man. The language of putting off and putting on is like changing clothing: the old identity has been removed and a new one given. Paul uses the past tense for both — this has already happened in conversion, not something still to be achieved.
elements.questionHint: Paul uses past tense for both actions — ‘have put off’ and ’have been clothed’ — rather than ’are putting off’ or ’should put on’. What does that tense tell you about when this change took place, and what does it mean for how believers should understand their identity?

elements.question: How is the new self being renewed?
elements.answerPlaceholder: Remember to answer the question from the passage
elements.sampleAnswer: The new self is being renewed in knowledge according to the image of its Creator. The renewal is an ongoing process (present tense — ‘is being renewed’), directed toward a specific goal: conformity to God’s image. It is a knowledge-shaped renewal, meaning that growing understanding of God and his ways is part of how the transformation takes place.
elements.questionHint: Paul says the new self ’is being renewed’ — an ongoing process — and gives both the means (‘in knowledge’) and the goal (‘according to the image of the one who created it’). What does the goal tell you about what genuine spiritual growth is actually moving toward?

elements.passageText: <sup>12</sup>So then, my dear friends, just as you have always obeyed, not only in my presence but even more in my absence, continue working out your salvation with awe and reverence, <sup>13</sup>for the one bringing forth in you both the desire and the effort — for the sake of his good pleasure — is God.

elements.question: What are believers commanded to do?
elements.answerPlaceholder: Remember to answer the question from the passage
elements.sampleAnswer: Believers are commanded to continue working out their salvation with awe and reverence. This is an active, ongoing command — not a passive waiting for God to do everything. The phrase ’working out’ suggests bringing to full expression or completion what is already present within. And it is to be done with seriousness: with awe and reverence, not casually.
elements.questionHint: Paul commands believers to ’work out’ their salvation — not ’work for’ it. What is the difference between those two phrases, and what does ’work out’ suggest about what is already true of the person being addressed?

elements.question: How does this fit with the reason Paul gives in v13?
elements.answerPlaceholder: Remember to answer the question from the passage
elements.sampleAnswer: Verse 13 gives the reason for the command in verse 12: God is at work in believers, producing both the desire and the effort. Far from making human effort pointless, this is the very foundation that makes it possible and necessary. We work, because God is working in us. Our effort is not self-generated willpower but the expression of God’s prior and continuing work. The two — human striving and divine working — are not in competition but are held together: we work precisely because he works.
elements.questionHint: Verse 13 begins with ’for’ — meaning it gives a reason. If God is producing both the desire and the effort in us, does that make the command in verse 12 unnecessary, or does it make it more possible? What does that logic tell you about how God’s work and our effort relate?

elements.text: God works in us – and we work. Both are true, and neither cancels out the other. The fact that God is at work in you is not a reason to be passive. It is the reason you can dare to try.

elements.passageText: <sup>21</sup>The person who has my commandments and obeys them is the one who loves me. The one who loves me will be loved by my Father, and I will love him and will reveal myself to him."

elements.question: What is the relationship between love and obedience?
elements.answerPlaceholder: Remember to answer the question from the passage
elements.sampleAnswer: Jesus presents love as the foundation of obedience and obedience as the evidence of love. The one who truly loves Jesus will obey his commandments — not as a burden but as an expression of love. And the promise attached is remarkable: the one who loves Jesus will be loved by the Father and will have Christ reveal himself to them. Obedience without love is mere performance; love without obedience is mere sentiment.
elements.questionHint: Jesus says the one who loves him ’has’ his commandments and ’obeys’ them — both possessing and keeping them. What does that sequence suggest about the relationship between knowing what Jesus commands and actually doing it out of love?

elements.question: Why does love change the nature of obedience?
elements.answerPlaceholder: Remember to answer the question from the passage
elements.sampleAnswer: When obedience flows from love rather than fear or obligation, the same actions have an entirely different character. Obeying someone you love is fundamentally unlike obeying a rule or avoiding punishment. Jesus frames it as the natural expression of a relationship — ‘the one who loves me will... obey.’ Love changes the motive, which changes the nature of the obedience itself: it becomes responsive rather than reluctant, personal rather than merely legal.
elements.questionHint: Think about the difference between doing something because you fear consequences and doing it because you love the person who asked — the action might look the same from the outside. What does Jesus’ framing suggest about what makes obedience distinctively Christian?

elements.text: There is a right way to fear God. But that is not what drives Christian obedience at its heart. Christian obedience is driven by love for Christ. That is a different engine entirely.

elements.passageText: <sup>12</sup>Therefore do not let sin reign in your mortal body so that you obey its desires, <sup>13</sup>and do not present your members to sin as instruments to be used for unrighteousness, but present yourselves to God as those who are alive from the dead and your members to God as instruments to be used for righteousness. <sup>14</sup>For sin will have no mastery over you, because you are not under law but under grace.

elements.question: What command does Paul give regarding sin? And why?
elements.answerPlaceholder: Remember to answer the question from the passage
elements.sampleAnswer: Paul commands believers not to let sin reign in their mortal bodies, and not to present their bodies to sin as instruments for unrighteousness — but instead to present themselves to God as those alive from the dead. The reason is given in verse 14: sin will have no mastery over them, because they are not under law but under grace. The command is grounded in a new status: they are alive from the dead, and grace — not sin’s dominion — defines their situation.
elements.questionHint: Paul’s command in verse 12 is ’do not let sin reign’ — not ’make sure you never sin’. What does the word ’reign’ imply about sin’s possible role in a believer’s life, and how does verse 14 give grounds for resisting it?

elements.question: What does it mean to be ’not under law but under grace’?
elements.answerPlaceholder: Remember to answer the question from the passage
elements.sampleAnswer: To be under law is to be in a system where standing before God depends on one’s own obedience — where failure means condemnation and sin has ultimate power to define your status. To be under grace is to be in a different system entirely: standing before God is secured by Christ’s work, not one’s own performance. This is Paul’s explanation for why sin no longer has mastery: its power to condemn has been broken, because the believer is no longer judged by law-keeping but received by grace.
elements.questionHint: Paul says being ’under law’ and ’under grace’ are alternative systems — you are in one or the other. What would being ’under law’ mean for someone who sins, and how does being ’under grace’ change what sin’s failure means for the believer?

elements.text: Conversion does not eliminate temptation; it breaks sin’s rule.

elements.passageText: <sup>12</sup>See to it, brothers and sisters, that none of you has an evil, unbelieving heart that forsakes the living God. <sup>13</sup>But exhort one another each day, as long as it is called "Today," that none of you may become hardened by sin’s deception. <sup>14</sup>For we have become partners with Christ, if in fact we hold our initial confidence firm until the end.

elements.question: What warning is given to believers?
elements.answerPlaceholder: Remember to answer the question from the passage
elements.sampleAnswer: The warning is against developing an evil, unbelieving heart that turns away from the living God. Specifically, sin’s deception is identified as the mechanism by which hearts become hardened over time. The warning is addressed to ’brothers and sisters’ — it is a warning for those within the community, not just outsiders. The danger of gradual, sin-induced hardening of heart is presented as a real possibility that requires active vigilance.
elements.questionHint: The warning mentions ’sin’s deception’ as the cause of hardening — not sin’s power. What does the word ’deception’ suggest about how this drift typically happens, and why that makes it particularly dangerous?

elements.question: What role do fellow believers play in perseverance?
elements.answerPlaceholder: Remember to answer the question from the passage
elements.sampleAnswer: Fellow believers play an active, daily role in perseverance: they are to exhort one another each day — not weekly, not occasionally, but daily — specifically to guard against the hardening that sin’s deception produces over time. The mutuality is significant: it is ’one another’, not just leaders exhorting congregation members. Perseverance is presented as a community project, not a solo effort.
elements.questionHint: The writer says ’exhort one another each day’ — why each day specifically, rather than weekly or occasionally? What does the frequency suggest about the speed at which hearts can drift, and why communal encouragement is necessary?

elements.text: Endurance doesn’t cause salvation, but it is evidence of it.

elements.passageText: <sup>18</sup>But the path of the righteous is like the bright morning light, growing brighter and brighter until full day.

elements.question: What image is used to describe the path of the righteous? How does this image capture the process of spiritual growth?
elements.answerPlaceholder: Remember to answer the question from the passage
elements.sampleAnswer: The image is of the morning light — beginning dimly and growing brighter and brighter until full day. This captures spiritual growth as a gradual, cumulative, and directional process. Light at dawn is real even when it is faint; the righteous path is genuine even when growth feels slow. The movement is always toward greater brightness, not oscillating randomly. And the end point — ‘full day’ — is certain and glorious, even if the present moment is still early morning.
elements.questionHint: At dawn, light is genuine even when it is still faint — you cannot yet see everything clearly. How does that aspect of the image speak to someone who is genuinely converted but feels their growth is too slow or too dim to be real?

elements.text:
Growth in holiness is often gradual, like a sunrise that you don’t notice moment by moment, but which has filled the sky by the time you look up. You may not feel like you are changing. But look back over five years, and ask honestly: are your desires different? Is sin more uncomfortable than it used to be? Do you love what you once ignored?

The same grace that brought you to Christ is at work in you now, reshaping you from the inside. You are not meant to do this by willpower alone – the Spirit is in you for exactly this purpose. Where growth feels painfully slow, don’t give up and don’t pretend. Bring it honestly to God. He who began a good work in you has not abandoned it. Christians are not perfect – but because of what Christ has done, and is doing, they are genuinely, lastingly new.
Questions for Further Reflection

elements.question: Is there a way in which you have been trying to produce fruit – better behaviour, more kindness – without attending to the roots? What would attending to the roots look like for you now?
elements.answerPlaceholder: Your reflections...

elements.question: Which aspect of the fruit of the Spirit do you most long to see grow in your life, and which do people who know you well most noticeably see lacking? Are those the same thing, or different?
elements.answerPlaceholder: Your reflections...

elements.question: Is there an area of your life where growth has felt slow or stalled? Have you been tempted to give up, or to stop thinking about it? What would it mean to bring that area before God?
elements.answerPlaceholder: Your reflections...

elements.text:
David had been a Christian – or thought he had – for fifteen years. He had been baptised, led a small group, and even preached occasionally. Then, in a period of serious illness, he started to ask himself honest questions. He had all the right answers. But when he looked at his life, he couldn’t find much that had actually changed. He had kept the outward form of Christianity without much of the inner reality.

The Bible speaks honestly to anyone who finds themselves asking David’s question – whether the answer turns out to be reassuring or unsettling. It offers both comfort and warning, sometimes in the same passage.

elements.question:
• What do you think of when you hear this word?
  • What do you think it really means?
  • Think of your own answer first, then look at ours.
elements.answer:
Some assume that doubting your salvation is spiritual humility – that being too sure means you’re taking grace for granted. Others assume that saying ’I’m saved’ is simply what Christians say, regardless of what their life shows.


Neither is quite right. Biblical assurance isn’t presumption or performance – it’s a confidence grounded in what Christ has done, witnessed by the Spirit, and evidenced by genuine change.

elements.passageText: <sup>11</sup>And every priest stands day after day serving and offering the same sacrifices again and again — sacrifices that can never remove sins. <sup>12</sup>But when this priest had offered one sacrifice for sins for all time, he sat down at the right hand of God, <sup>13</sup>where he is now waiting until his enemies are made a footstool for his feet. <sup>14</sup>For by one offering he has perfected for all time those who are made holy.

elements.question: What did Jesus achieve, and what confidence does this give us?
elements.answerPlaceholder: Remember to answer the question from the passage
elements.sampleAnswer: Jesus offered one sacrifice for sins — sufficient for all time — and then sat down, signalling completion. Unlike the priests who stood day after day offering sacrifices that could never remove sins, Christ’s single offering has perfected for all time those who are made holy. The confidence this gives is immense: the work is finished. There is nothing more to be added and nothing that can undo it. Assurance rests on his completed work, not our ongoing performance.
elements.questionHint: The passage draws a deliberate contrast between the priests who ’stand day after day’ and Christ who ’sat down’ — what does the act of sitting down, after offering his sacrifice, communicate about the status of the work he came to do?

elements.text: Christians are not meant to live in constant uncertainty about their relationship with God. Our confidence begins – and ends – with what Christ has done. Not with how we feel.

elements.passageText: <sup>15</sup>For you did not receive the spirit of slavery leading again to fear, but you received the Spirit of adoption, by whom we cry, ‘Abba, Father.’ <sup>16</sup>The Spirit himself bears witness to our spirit that we are God’s children.

elements.question: What testimony does the Spirit give?
elements.answerPlaceholder: Remember to answer the question from the passage
elements.sampleAnswer: The Spirit himself bears witness to our spirit that we are God’s children. This is a direct, personal testimony — the Spirit of adoption working within the believer to produce the cry ’Abba, Father’, which is itself evidence of the relationship. The Spirit does not merely inform us of a legal fact; he creates the experience of sonship that confirms it from the inside.
elements.questionHint: Paul says we received ’the Spirit of adoption’ rather than ’a spirit of slavery’. What does the word ’adoption’ tell you about the kind of relationship the Spirit is testifying to — and what kind of confidence that relationship should produce?

elements.passageText: 3Now by this we know that we have come to know God: if we keep his commandments. 4The one who says ‘I have come to know God’ and yet does not keep his commandments is a liar, and the truth is not in such a person. 5But whoever obeys his word, truly the love of God has been perfected in him. By this we know that we are in him: the one who says he resides in God ought himself to walk just as Jesus walked.

elements.question: According to John, how do we know that we know God?
elements.answerPlaceholder: Remember to answer the question from the passage
elements.sampleAnswer: We know that we know God if we keep his commandments. John makes this the test: keeping God’s commandments is evidence of genuine knowledge of God. And he rules out the alternative explicitly — anyone who claims to know God but does not keep his commandments is a liar. Genuine knowledge of God produces obedience; claimed knowledge without obedience is self-deception.
elements.questionHint: John says ’by this we know’ — he is giving a test. What is the test, and what does he say about the person who claims to know God but fails it?

elements.text: The Spirit also testifies that we belong to God. And assurance grows as we see God’s work in our lives. Changed lives do not earn salvation. But they are evidence that salvation is real.

elements.passageText: <sup>3</sup>Blessed be the God and Father of our Lord Jesus Christ! By his great mercy he gave us new birth into a living hope through the resurrection of Jesus Christ from the dead, <sup>4</sup>that is, into an inheritance imperishable, undefiled, and unfading. It is reserved in heaven for you, <sup>5</sup>who are being protected by God’s power through faith for a salvation ready to be revealed in the last time.

elements.question: What hope, and what inheritance, has God given believers?
elements.answerPlaceholder: Remember to answer the question from the passage
elements.sampleAnswer: God has given believers a living hope through the resurrection of Jesus Christ — a hope that is alive and certain because its basis (the resurrection) is a historical reality. The inheritance is described with three negatives: imperishable (it cannot decay), undefiled (it cannot be corrupted), and unfading (it cannot lose its quality). It is reserved in heaven — held securely for them, not dependent on their ability to hold onto it.
elements.questionHint: Peter describes the inheritance with three ’un-’ words — imperishable, undefiled, unfading. What do those three negatives together rule out, and what does that tell you about the security of what God has reserved for his people?

elements.question: How are believers being guarded until the final salvation?
elements.answerPlaceholder: Remember to answer the question from the passage
elements.sampleAnswer: Believers are being protected by God’s power through faith. The guarding is actively God’s — it is his power that protects. And it operates through faith — faith is the means by which we remain connected to that protection. This double security (an inheritance reserved in heaven AND God’s power guarding the believer on earth) means that both ends of the journey are secured: the destination is held, and the traveller is kept.
elements.questionHint: Peter says believers are ’being protected by God’s power’ — the guarding is ongoing and active. Who is doing the protecting, and what does that tell you about whether the security of believers ultimately depends on the strength of their own grip?

elements.text: True believers continue. Not because they are strong, but because God keeps them. He does not only save his people. He holds them.

elements.passageText: <sup>21</sup>And you were at one time strangers and enemies in your minds as expressed through your evil deeds, <sup>22</sup>but now he has reconciled you by his physical body through death to present you holy, without blemish, and blameless before him — <sup>23</sup>if indeed you remain in the faith, established and firm, without shifting from the hope of the gospel that you heard.

elements.question: What change has taken place for believers?
elements.answerPlaceholder: Remember to answer the question from the passage
elements.sampleAnswer: A radical change of status has taken place. Believers were formerly strangers and enemies — estranged from God and actively hostile to him in mind and deed. But now, through Christ’s death, they have been reconciled. The goal of this reconciliation is to present them holy, without blemish, and blameless before God. From enemy to blameless: the transformation is complete in its scope, even if ongoing in its application.
elements.questionHint: Paul uses the word ’enemies’ to describe the former status of these believers — not just strangers or indifferent, but enemies. What does the use of that particular word tell you about how dramatic the change that reconciliation brings actually is?

elements.question: What does it mean to remain ’stable and steadfast’?
elements.answerPlaceholder: Remember to answer the question from the passage
elements.sampleAnswer: To remain stable and steadfast (the NET uses ’established and firm’) means to stay grounded in the faith and not shift from the hope of the gospel. The opposite of this is drifting — moving away from the gospel that was heard. Remaining does not mean achieving a static, effortless state but actively holding to the same foundation: the hope of the gospel. The image is of something firmly planted rather than something rootless and easily blown about.
elements.questionHint: Paul describes the opposite of remaining as ’shifting from the hope of the gospel’ — what does the word ’shifting’ suggest about how departure from faith typically happens: suddenly, or gradually?

elements.text: Believers are repeatedly urged to continue. Perseverance does not earn salvation. It shows that salvation is real.

elements.passageText: <sup>23</sup>And let us hold unwaveringly to the hope we confess, for the one who made the promise is trustworthy. <sup>24</sup>And let us take thought of how to spur one another on to love and good works, <sup>25</sup>not abandoning our own meetings, as some are in the habit of doing, but encouraging each other, and even more so because you see the day drawing near.

elements.question: What role does meeting together play in perseverance?
elements.answerPlaceholder: Remember to answer the question from the passage
elements.sampleAnswer: Meeting together is presented as essential to perseverance, not optional. The text warns against ’abandoning our own meetings’ — and the reason given is that gathering enables believers to spur one another on to love and good works, and to encourage each other. Without this mutual encouragement, the drift that sin’s deception causes goes unchecked. And the approaching ’day’ — the day of judgment — makes this all the more urgent, not less.
elements.questionHint: The writer says we should not abandon meeting together, ‘even more so because you see the day drawing near.’ Why would the imminence of the day of judgment make meeting together more important rather than less?

elements.text: God uses the Christian community to sustain believers. Christians are not meant to persevere alone.

elements.passageText: <sup>11</sup>‘Now the parable means this: The seed is the word of God. <sup>12</sup>Those along the path are the ones who have heard; then the devil comes and takes away the word from their hearts, so that they may not believe and be saved. <sup>13</sup>Those on the rock are the ones who receive the word with joy when they hear it, but they have no root. They believe for a while, but in a time of testing fall away. <sup>14</sup>As for the seed that fell among thorns, these are the ones who hear, but as they go on their way they are choked by the worries and riches and pleasures of life, and their fruit does not ripen. <sup>15</sup>But as for the seed that landed on good soil, these are the ones who, after hearing the word, cling to it with an honest and good heart, and bear fruit with steadfast endurance.

elements.question: Why does seed on the rocky ground fail, but grow in good soil?
elements.answerPlaceholder: Remember to answer the question from the passage
elements.sampleAnswer: The rocky-ground hearers receive the word with joy and believe for a while — but they have no root, and when testing comes they fall away. The problem is not initial enthusiasm but lack of depth. By contrast, the good-soil hearers cling to the word with an honest and good heart, and bear fruit with steadfast endurance. The key differences are: depth of reception, quality of heart, and sustained perseverance rather than only initial response.
elements.questionHint: Both the rocky-ground hearers and the good-soil hearers hear the word — but Jesus describes them very differently in verse 13 and verse 15. What is the specific difference between how each type of hearer responds over time, especially when things get hard?

elements.text: Scripture is honest: not every response to the gospel is genuine. Some fall away. The soil matters.

elements.passageText: <sup>16</sup>They profess to know God but with their deeds they deny him, since they are detestable, disobedient, and unfit for any good deed.

elements.question: Why is this warning important for the church?
elements.answerPlaceholder: Remember to answer the question from the passage
elements.sampleAnswer: The warning is important because it shows that profession and practice can come apart entirely — and when they do, the profession counts for nothing. People can use the right language (‘I know God’) while their deeds tell a contradictory story. The church needs to know this so it is not deceived by words alone, and so individuals within it can honestly examine whether their own lives match their profession. The standard Paul applies is searching: not just occasional failure, but being ’detestable, disobedient, and unfit for any good deed.’
elements.questionHint: Paul says these people ’profess’ to know God but ’deny’ him with their deeds — what does the word ’deny’ suggest about the relationship between a person’s actions and what those actions are actually communicating about their beliefs?

elements.text: Words alone do not prove genuine faith. Anyone can say the right things. What cannot be faked, over time, is a transformed life.

elements.passageText: <sup>21</sup>‘Not everyone who says to me, ‘Lord, Lord,‘ will enter into the kingdom of heaven — only the one who does the will of my Father in heaven. <sup>22</sup>On that day, many will say to me, ‘Lord, Lord, didn’t we prophesy in your name, and in your name cast out demons and do many powerful deeds?’ <sup>23</sup>Then I will declare to them, ‘I never knew you. Go away from me, you lawbreakers!’

elements.question: What does Matthew say about the danger of false assurance?
elements.answerPlaceholder: Remember to answer the question from the passage
elements.sampleAnswer: Jesus warns that many — not a few — will appeal to him on the day of judgment on the basis of their religious activity (prophesying, casting out demons, powerful deeds), only to hear ’I never knew you.’ The danger of false assurance is therefore not limited to nominal Christians; it extends to those with impressive religious involvement. The test that matters is not activity or language but whether Christ knows them — that is, whether there is a real relationship expressed in doing the Father’s will.
elements.questionHint: The people Jesus describes have done remarkable things in his name — yet he says ’I never knew you.’ What does Jesus’ use of the word ’knew’ suggest about what genuine faith actually involves, beyond activity and correct language?

elements.text: You can be outwardly religious, and yet Christ doesn’t know you. That is a terrifying possibility. Religious activity isn’t the same as knowing Christ.

elements.passageText: <sup>5</sup>Put yourselves to the test to see if you are in the faith; examine yourselves! Or do you not recognize regarding yourselves that Jesus Christ is in you — unless, indeed, you fail the test!

elements.question: What does Paul tell believers to do?
elements.answerPlaceholder: Remember to answer the question from the passage
elements.sampleAnswer: Paul tells believers to test and examine themselves — specifically to see whether they are in the faith. He frames this as something they should be able to recognise: Jesus Christ is in them, unless they fail the test. The command is serious and self-directed: not ’examine others’ but ’examine yourselves.’ Self-examination is not presented as morbid introspection but as a responsible, expected part of the Christian life.
elements.questionHint: Paul uses two words in quick succession — ‘test’ and ’examine’ — both directed at the self. What does the doubling of these words suggest about the seriousness with which he expects believers to undertake this self-assessment?

elements.text:
This chapter holds two things together; don’t let go of either. If you are trusting in Christ – even weakly, even with doubts – you have real grounds for assurance. Not because your faith is strong, but because the One you are trusting is. His death is sufficient. His Spirit witnesses to your adoption. The changed desires you notice in yourself, however imperfect, are evidence of his work. You do not have to live in constant uncertainty. You are held.

But the Bible is also honest: not every profession of faith is real. Religious activity, church attendance, and correct answers are not the same as knowing Christ. If something in this chapter has unsettled you – if the warning passages landed more heavily than the assurance passages – don’t brush that aside. Bring it to God. Ask him to show you whether what you have is real, and to make it real if it’s not. That prayer will not go unanswered. He does not turn away anyone who comes to him in genuine need.

The goal of self-examination is not despair. It is to drive you – or bring you back – to Christ himself. That is always the right destination.
Questions for Further Reflection

elements.question: When you think about your own standing before God, do you struggle more with false assurance (‘I’m fine’) or with doubt (‘I’m probably not saved’)? What feeds that tendency in you?
elements.answerPlaceholder: Your reflections...

elements.question: 2 Corinthians 13:5 says, ‘Examine yourselves to see whether you are in the faith.’ As you do that honestly: what do you find? What gives you confidence – and what gives you pause?
elements.answerPlaceholder: Your reflections...

elements.question: Is there anything in this chapter (a warning, a passage, a description of false faith) that has unsettled you? Bring it honestly to God: is this the Spirit convicting you toward genuine faith, or the enemy trying to undermine real assurance? How can you tell the difference?
elements.answerPlaceholder: Your reflections...

elements.text:
Suppose you were a doctor, and you were sent to an epidemic with a cure for a deadly disease. Your task is not to design clever advertising campaigns or repackage the medicine to make it fashionable. People’s lives depend on something far simpler: that the cure be clearly explained and offered.

The gospel is like that. It is not a product that needs clever marketing. It is the announcement of what God has done in Christ to save sinners. When Christians share the gospel, our task is not to sell something attractive, but to faithfully tell the truth about the cure God has provided.

elements.question:
• What do you think of when you hear this word?
  • What do you think it really means?
  • Think of your own answer first, then look at ours.
elements.answer:
The word ’evangelism’ tends to conjure images of confrontational strangers, high-pressure tactics, or slick presentations. But the word simply means announcing good news.


The task isn’t to sell, persuade, or close a deal – it’s to tell the truth clearly about what God has done in Christ, summon people to respond, and trust God to do his work. Less salesperson. More witness.

elements.passageText: <sup>42</sup>And every day both in the temple courts and from house to house, they did not stop teaching and proclaiming the good news that Jesus is the Christ.

elements.question: What did the apostles continually do, and where did they do it?
elements.answerPlaceholder: Remember to answer the question from the passage
elements.sampleAnswer: The apostles continually taught and proclaimed the good news that Jesus is the Christ — and they did not stop. The locations were both public (the temple courts) and private (from house to house), indicating that their proclamation was not confined to formal settings but was woven into the whole fabric of their daily life. The word ’every day’ underlines the consistency and persistence of their witness.
elements.questionHint: The verse mentions both a public location and a private one — what does proclaiming the good news in both the temple courts and from house to house suggest about whether evangelism belongs only in formal or official settings?

elements.question: Who was the focus of their message?
elements.answerPlaceholder: Remember to answer the question from the passage
elements.sampleAnswer: The focus of their message was Jesus — specifically, the proclamation that Jesus is the Christ. Their teaching and their good news both centred on a person and his identity. The gospel is not a set of principles or a way of life abstracted from a person; it is news about who Jesus is.
elements.questionHint: The verse ends with a specific claim about Jesus — ‘that Jesus is the Christ.’ What does that specific content tell you about what the apostles believed had to be central to any true proclamation of the good news?

elements.passageText: <sup>1</sup>When I came to you, brothers and sisters, I did not come with superior eloquence or wisdom as I proclaimed the testimony of God. <sup>2</sup>For I decided to be concerned about nothing among you except Jesus Christ, and him crucified.

elements.question: What did Paul emphasise when preaching in Corinth?
elements.answerPlaceholder: Remember to answer the question from the passage
elements.sampleAnswer: Paul emphasised Jesus Christ, and him crucified — nothing else. He deliberately decided to be concerned about nothing else among the Corinthians. This was a conscious choice: he set aside superior eloquence and human wisdom, and centred everything on the crucified Christ. The cross, not rhetorical sophistication, was the content and power of his message.
elements.questionHint: Paul says he ’decided’ to be concerned about nothing except Christ crucified — it was a deliberate choice, not a default. What does that word ’decided’ suggest about the temptations he was aware of when preaching in a sophisticated city like Corinth?

elements.question: What danger does this passage warn against in evangelism?
elements.answerPlaceholder: Remember to answer the question from the passage
elements.sampleAnswer: The danger is of substituting clever presentation — superior eloquence or human wisdom — for the simple proclamation of Christ crucified. In a culture that valued rhetorical skill, the temptation would have been to make the message more persuasive or impressive through technique.
