But Paul’s point is that the power is not in the delivery; it is in the message itself. Prioritising eloquence risks obscuring or displacing the cross, which is the actual content and power of the gospel.
elements.questionHint: Paul contrasts his approach (‘not with superior eloquence or wisdom’) with what he chose to focus on (‘Jesus Christ, and him crucified’). What would be lost if eloquence and rhetorical skill became the primary tool rather than the message of the cross?

elements.text: At the centre of evangelism is a person: Jesus Christ. Not our story. Not our moral advice. Not our religious experience. Christ – who he is, and what he has done.

elements.passageText: <sup>26</sup>Was it not necessary for the Christ to suffer these things and enter into his glory?’ <sup>27</sup>Then beginning with Moses and all the prophets, he interpreted to them the things written about himself in all the scriptures.

elements.question: What does Jesus explain to the disciples?
elements.answerPlaceholder: Remember to answer the question from the passage
elements.sampleAnswer: Jesus explains that it was necessary for the Christ to suffer and then enter into his glory — and he grounds this in all the Scriptures, working through Moses and all the prophets to show what was written about himself. He is showing that his death and resurrection were not unexpected failures but the fulfilment of what Scripture had promised all along. The entire Old Testament, he teaches, is pointing to him.
elements.questionHint: Jesus says ’beginning with Moses and all the prophets’ he interpreted what was written about himself — what does the scope (‘all the scriptures’) and the starting point (‘Moses’) suggest about how much of the Bible he understood to be about him?

elements.question: What is the role of Scripture in understanding Christ?
elements.answerPlaceholder: Remember to answer the question from the passage
elements.sampleAnswer: Scripture is the means by which Christ is properly understood — it is the interpretive framework through which his suffering and glory make sense. Jesus himself uses Scripture to explain himself: he does not bypass it or supplement it with new teaching, but opens it to show what was always there. For evangelism this means that the Scriptures are not optional background — they are the lens through which people can rightly understand who Jesus is and why he came.
elements.questionHint: Jesus could have simply told the disciples directly what had happened and why. Instead he ’interpreted to them the things written about himself in all the scriptures.’ Why go via Scripture rather than simply explaining it without it?

elements.passageText: <sup>2</sup>Paul went to the Jews in the synagogue, as he customarily did, and on three Sabbath days he addressed them from the scriptures, <sup>3</sup>explaining and demonstrating that the Christ had to suffer and to rise from the dead, saying, ‘This Jesus I am proclaiming to you is the Christ.’

elements.question: What method does Paul use? What does he conclude about Jesus?
elements.answerPlaceholder: Remember to answer the question from the passage
elements.sampleAnswer: Paul’s method is to address his audience from the Scriptures — opening and demonstrating from the text, not merely asserting. He explains and demonstrates that the Christ had to suffer and rise from the dead. His conclusion is a direct identification: ‘This Jesus I am proclaiming to you is the Christ.’ Scripture is used as evidence; the conclusion is a specific claim about Jesus’ identity.
elements.questionHint: Paul uses two words to describe his approach — ‘explaining’ and ’demonstrating’. What does the word ’demonstrating’ add to mere explanation, and what does it suggest about his relationship to the Scriptures he was using?

elements.text: The gospel is rooted in God’s word. We didn’t invent it. We open Scripture, and show where it points: always, from every angle, to Christ.

elements.passageText: 19Therefore repent and turn back so that your sins may be wiped out, so that times of refreshing may come from the presence of the Lord.

elements.question: What command, and what promise, does Peter give?
elements.answerPlaceholder: Remember to answer the question from the passage
elements.sampleAnswer: Peter commands: repent and turn back. The promise attached is twofold: sins will be wiped out, and times of refreshing will come from the presence of the Lord. The structure is ’do this, so that this may happen’ — the command and the promise are connected. Peter is not merely informing his audience but summoning them to a response with specific consequences.
elements.questionHint: The verse has the structure of ’command, so that promise’ — what is the relationship between the command Peter gives and the two things he promises will follow from it?

elements.question: What does this tell us about the urgency of the gospel?
elements.answerPlaceholder: Remember to answer the question from the passage
elements.sampleAnswer: The gospel is urgent because real and significant consequences hang on the response. Sins being wiped out and refreshing from the Lord’s presence are not trivial benefits — they are the difference between guilt remaining and being forgiven, between spiritual exhaustion and renewal. If these things are on offer through repentance and turning back, then the call to do so is not something that can be treated casually or deferred indefinitely.
elements.questionHint: Peter says ’repent... so that your sins may be wiped out.’ What is at stake if someone does not repent — and what does that imply about whether the gospel can be treated as interesting information rather than an urgent summons?

elements.passageText: <sup>15</sup>He said, ‘The time is fulfilled and the kingdom of God is near. Repent and believe the gospel!’

elements.question: What announcement is made? What responses are called for?
elements.answerPlaceholder: Remember to answer the question from the passage
elements.sampleAnswer: The announcement is: the time is fulfilled and the kingdom of God is near. This is a declaration about what God has done and what has now arrived. The responses called for are repentance and belief in the gospel — both commands, both in response to the arrival of the kingdom. The gospel is presented as news that demands a response, not merely information to be filed away.
elements.questionHint: Jesus first makes an announcement, then issues commands — what is the logical connection between the announcement (‘the time is fulfilled, the kingdom is near’) and the commands that follow (‘repent and believe’)? Why does the arrival of the kingdom require an immediate response?

elements.text: Evangelism does not stop at explanation. It calls for a response. The gospel is not information to consider. It is a summons to obey.

elements.passageText: <sup>11</sup>Therefore, because we know the fear of the Lord, we try to persuade people. We are well known to God, and I hope we are well known to your consciences too.

elements.question: Why is evangelism serious? What should motivate us?
elements.answerPlaceholder: Remember to answer the question from the passage
elements.sampleAnswer: Evangelism is serious because it is carried out in the knowledge of the fear of the Lord — an awareness of God’s holiness and judgment. This is what motivates Paul to try to persuade people. The fear of the Lord is not panic but a settled recognition of who God is and what is at stake. Evangelism motivated by God’s character and the reality of judgment is serious and sustained in a way that evangelism motivated merely by obligation or enthusiasm is not.
elements.questionHint: Paul says ’because we know the fear of the Lord, we try to persuade people’ — the fear of the Lord is given as the reason for evangelism. What does that motivation reveal about what Paul understood to be at stake for the people he was trying to reach?

elements.passageText: <sup>2</sup>Instead we have renounced shameful hidden deeds, not behaving with deceptiveness or distorting the word of God, but by open proclamation of the truth we commend ourselves to everyone’s conscience before God.

elements.question: What methods does Paul reject?
elements.answerPlaceholder: Remember to answer the question from the passage
elements.sampleAnswer: Paul rejects three things: shameful hidden deeds, deceptiveness, and distorting the word of God. Instead he commits to open proclamation of the truth. The rejected methods all involve some form of concealment or manipulation — presenting the message in a way that obscures, distorts, or deceives. The contrast is between transparent truth-telling and any technique that relies on managing or manipulating the audience.
elements.questionHint: Paul lists three things he has ’renounced’ — look at what they have in common. What do shameful hidden deeds, deceptiveness, and distorting the word all involve, and how does ’open proclamation of the truth’ stand in contrast to all of them?

elements.text: The apostles urged people to believe — but without manipulation, without pressure, without tricks. Persuasion is legitimate. Pressure is not. The difference matters.

elements.passageText: <sup>8</sup>And when he comes, he will prove the world wrong concerning sin and righteousness and judgment — <sup>9</sup>concerning sin, because they do not believe in me; <sup>10</sup>concerning righteousness, because I am going to the Father and you will see me no longer; <sup>11</sup>and concerning judgment, because the ruler of this world has been condemned.

elements.question: What work does the Spirit perform in the world?
elements.answerPlaceholder: Remember to answer the question from the passage
elements.sampleAnswer: The Spirit proves the world wrong — that is, he convicts or exposes — concerning three things: sin (the world’s failure to believe in Jesus), righteousness (Christ’s vindication by the Father), and judgment (the condemnation of the ruler of this world). This is the Spirit’s own work of internal conviction that no human preacher can produce. The evangelist speaks; the Spirit convicts.
elements.questionHint: Jesus says the Spirit will ’prove the world wrong’ about sin, righteousness, and judgment — these are not things the preacher can produce in the listener through better arguments. What does that tell you about whose work genuine conviction is, and what that means for the evangelist’s responsibility?

elements.passageText: <sup>4</sup>We know, brothers and sisters loved by God, that he has chosen you, <sup>5</sup>in that our gospel did not come to you merely in words, but in power and in the Holy Spirit and with deep conviction (surely you recall the character we displayed when we were among you for your benefit).

elements.question: What was the Spirit’s role in the gospel coming to Thessalonica?
elements.answerPlaceholder: Remember to answer the question from the passage
elements.sampleAnswer: The gospel came to the Thessalonians not merely in words but in power, in the Holy Spirit, and with deep conviction. The Spirit’s role was to accompany the words with power — making the message effective in a way that human speech alone cannot achieve. Paul links the Thessalonians’ election (God having chosen them) with the Spirit’s powerful working when the gospel arrived. The Spirit is what makes the gospel more than mere information.
elements.questionHint: Paul distinguishes between the gospel coming ’merely in words’ and coming ’in power and in the Holy Spirit’. What is the difference between those two, and what does it suggest about what has to happen for words about Christ to actually change someone?

elements.question: Because conversion is God’s work, what does this mean for our evangelism?
elements.answerPlaceholder: Remember to answer the question from the passage
elements.sampleAnswer: Because conversion is God’s work — accomplished by the Spirit who brings power and deep conviction — our evangelism is freed from the pressure to produce results through our own skill or persistence. We are responsible for faithful proclamation; God is responsible for conversion. This means we can speak without manipulation, leave results with God, and persevere even when our words seem to have no effect — trusting that the Spirit who came with power to Thessalonica can do the same anywhere.
elements.questionHint: If the gospel came to Thessalonica ’in power and in the Holy Spirit’ — and that power is not something the preacher generated — what is the preacher’s job, and what is God’s job? And how does knowing that distinction change the pressure you feel when sharing the gospel?

elements.text: Because conversion is God’s work, evangelism depends on the Spirit’s power. The Spirit opens hearts, brings conviction, gives new life. We cannot do any of that. What we can do is speak. So speak.

elements.passageText: 24And the Lord’s slave must not engage in heated disputes but be kind toward all, an apt teacher, patient, 25correcting opponents with gentleness. Perhaps God will grant them repentance and then knowledge of the truth and they will come to their senses and escape the devil’s trap where they are held captive to do his will.

elements.question: What kind of attitude should the Lord’s servant have?
elements.answerPlaceholder: Remember to answer the question from the passage
elements.sampleAnswer: The Lord’s servant must not engage in heated disputes but must be kind toward all, an apt teacher, patient, and correcting opponents with gentleness. These qualities are the character of the evangelist: not argumentative or combative but gentle, kind, and patient. This is not softness on the truth but firmness on the truth held with a gentle spirit toward those who oppose it.
elements.questionHint: Paul contrasts ’heated disputes’ with the qualities he lists — kindness, patience, gentleness. What does making that contrast suggest about the temptation that comes when someone opposes the gospel, and why gentleness is specifically necessary in those moments?

elements.question: Why does evangelism require patience?
elements.answerPlaceholder: Remember to answer the question from the passage
elements.sampleAnswer: Evangelism requires patience because repentance is something God grants — ‘perhaps God will grant them repentance.’ The word ’perhaps’ acknowledges that the timing and occurrence of repentance is in God’s hands, not ours. We do not control when or whether a person turns to God. People may oppose the truth for a long time before God grants repentance. Patience is therefore not optional but built into the nature of evangelism: we speak, we correct gently, and we wait on God.
elements.questionHint: Paul says ’perhaps God will grant them repentance’ — the word ’perhaps’ leaves the outcome open and places it in God’s hands. What does that word tell you about why the evangelist needs patience, and why trying to force or manufacture repentance is both futile and wrong?

elements.text:
Evangelism requires patience. God works in his own time, not ours. Our job is faithfulness, gentleness, and perseverance. The harvest is his.

The gospel isn’t a product that needs clever packaging. You are not a salesperson. You are someone who has received a cure for a fatal disease – and you know people who are dying. That changes things. It means your job is not to be impressive, or persuasive, or to close a deal. Your job is to be honest and faithful: to tell people clearly what God has done in Christ, to call them to repent and believe, and then to leave the results with the God who opens hearts.

The pressure is off you, because the work was never yours to do alone. As you finish this course, there is one question worth pondering: Who is the person God has put in front of you – a friend, a family member, a colleague – who needs to hear this? You don’t need a special gift or a perfect moment. You need willingness. The Spirit will do the rest.
Questions for Further Reflection

elements.question: Have you ever felt pressure – from yourself, from a church culture, or from a specific situation – to push someone toward a decision rather than simply tell them the truth? What happened? What would you do differently?
elements.answerPlaceholder: Your reflections...

elements.question: When you last had a conversation about faith with someone who isn’t a Christian, what did you talk about? Was Christ and what he has done central – or did you drift to something easier?
elements.answerPlaceholder: Your reflections...

elements.question: Be honest: does the thought of talking to someone about Jesus make you anxious? What specifically worries you most – rejection, getting it wrong, damaging a relationship? How does what you’ve learned in this chapter speak to that specific fear?
elements.answerPlaceholder: Your reflections...

studyOnboardingSlides.heading: Conversion: New not (just) Nice
studyOnboardingSlides.body:
A nine-session Bible study exploring what genuine conversion means – and why it matters.

Work through each chapter at your own pace, or use it with a group.

studyOnboardingSlides.heading: Read, reflect, write
studyOnboardingSlides.body:
Each chapter has Bible questions tied to specific passages, and personal reflection questions at the end.

Tap ⓘ beside any Bible reference to read the passage without leaving the app.

studyOnboardingSlides.heading: Saved on your device
studyOnboardingSlides.body:
Your answers are saved automatically as you type, and stored on this device.

Use the Share button at the bottom of each chapter to send your answers to a group leader or mentor.

studyOnboardingSlides.heading: How to use this app
studyOnboardingSlides.body: If you want a full guide to getting the most from this app and this study – whether on your own or in a group – you can tap the button below.

action.label: Go to the ‘How to use’ page →

studyOnboardingSlides.bodyAfter: You can also find it any time via the ? icon in the top navigation bar.

blocks.title: People who grew up in Christian circles
blocks.body: If you have been around church for most of your life, you may find that you have never stopped to ask a basic question: am I actually converted? You know the language, you know the stories, you may even know the Bible well. This study is designed to help you examine that question carefully and honestly — not to unsettle you, but to give you something solid to stand on.

blocks.title: People exploring Christianity
blocks.body: If you are not yet a Christian but are genuinely curious — wondering what Christianity actually involves, what it costs, and what it offers — this study is for you too. It doesn’t assume you’ve already made up your mind. It simply works through what the Bible says, and invites you to think carefully about it.

blocks.title: People discipling others
blocks.body: If you are walking alongside someone else in their faith — as a mentor, a small group leader, or a friend — this study gives you a structured way to work through the foundations of the Christian life together. The nine chapters cover the ground that matters most in the early stages of discipleship.

blocks.title: On your own
blocks.body: This study works well as a personal study. Work through each chapter at your own pace, read the Bible passages, write your answers to the questions, and meditate on the reflection questions at the end. There is no right speed — some chapters may take twenty minutes, others may take longer if a question stops you in your tracks.

blocks.title: In a group
blocks.body: The study also works well in a small group setting. You could work through each chapter together from scratch, reading the passages and discussing the questions as a group. Alternatively — and this is often the most rewarding approach — each person works through the chapter individually first, writes their answers, and then the group comes together to discuss what they found. Either way works. The questions are designed to prompt genuine reflection, not just right answers.

blocks.title: Working with a leader or mentor
blocks.body:
It can make a real difference to have someone guiding you through the study — a group leader, a pastor, or a more experienced Christian who can help you think through your answers and questions. Perhaps they are the one who recommended this study to you in the first place.

At the end of each chapter, the Share button lets you send your written answers directly to your leader or mentor — via WhatsApp, email, or any other app on your phone. This gives them a window into your thinking before you meet, and makes for much richer conversation.

blocks.title: Two kinds of questions
blocks.body: Each chapter has two kinds of questions. The Bible questions are tied to specific passages — asking you to look carefully at what the text says. The reflection questions at the end are more personal — asking what the chapter means for you specifically.

blocks.title: Common Misunderstanding boxes
blocks.body: Throughout the study you will encounter darker-coloured cards headed Common Misunderstanding. These address words — like grace, repentance, or faith — that Christians use all the time but that are often misunderstood. Take a moment to think about what you understand by the word, then tap Find out to see a short clarification.

intro: These notes are designed to help group leaders and mentors get the most out of each chapter – both for the people they are leading and for themselves. They are not a substitute for working through the material yourself first. The best leaders are those who have worked through the questions personally before they help others doing the same.

Create a safe space for honesty. The questions in this study are designed to prompt real reflection, not just correct answers. Your job is to make the group feel safe enough to say what they actually think, not what they think a Christian is supposed to say.
Don’t rush the silences. Some of the best moments in a group study happen after a question lands and nobody speaks for a few seconds. Resist the urge to fill every silence.
Watch for the person who goes quiet. Sometimes the most significant thing happening in the room is with the person who says the least. Check in with them afterwards.

keyPoints.body:
The opening chapter establishes the radical nature of conversion. It’s not just about becoming a ’nicer’ person or adding religious habits to an existing life. It’s about being made new from the inside out.


Draw out the contrast between Simon the Pharisee (who looked godly but wasn’t) and Zacchaeus (who looked ungodly but was made new). This helps the group see that conversion is God’s work, not ours.


The concept of new birth (John 3:1-7) is central. Use it to show that we don’t just need a moral tune-up; we need a completely new life that only God can provide.

pastorals.body:
Some in your group may have been ’in church’ for years but never truly experienced conversion. The Simon/Zacchaeus contrast can be very unsettling for them. Be gentle but clear: the gospel is for the ’nice’ person just as much as for the ’bad’ person.


Others may feel they aren’t ’good enough’ to be converted. Emphasise that Zacchaeus didn’t clean up his life before Jesus went to his house. Conversion is the start of the change, not the reward for it.

watch.body: 👁️ Watch for people who define conversion solely in terms of a specific emotional experience or a date on a calendar. While those are fine, the biblical emphasis is on the reality of the change, not just the moment of it.

keyPoints.body:
This is the ’bad news’ chapter that makes the ’good news’ meaningful. Unless we understand the depth of the problem, conversion will always seem like an overreaction.


Focus on the biblical diagnosis: we aren’t just ’struggling’ or ’confused’ — we are spiritually dead (Ephesians 2:1-3). Dead people can’t help themselves; they need a miracle.


Help the group see that sin is personal rebellion against God (Psalm 51:4). It’s not just breaking rules; it’s a broken relationship with our Creator.

pastorals.body:
This chapter can feel heavy. Some may feel crushed by the weight of the diagnosis. Your role is to ensure they see the diagnosis as a necessary step toward the cure. Don’t let the session end without pointing to the hope mentioned in the closing.


Be aware of those who have a very low view of themselves. They may hear ’you are the problem’ as a confirmation of their own self-hatred. Distinguish between legal guilt/spiritual death (the biblical diagnosis) and worthlessness (the enemy’s lie).

watch.body: 👁️ Watch for those who want to blame their sin on circumstances, upbringing, or other people. Gently bring them back to Jeremiah 17:9 — the problem is internal.

chapters.title: God, who is Rich in Mercy

keyPoints.body:
The focus shifts entirely to God’s initiative. Conversion happens because God moved first. This provides the ultimate ground for humility (we didn’t earn it) and confidence (God won’t lose us).


Emphasise substitution (2 Corinthians 5:21). The reason God can be ’rich in mercy’ to us is because Christ took the ’richness of wrath’ that we deserved. This is the heart of the gospel.


The ’Joy’ illustration helps show that God’s reaching out often precedes our awareness of it. It’s a great way to talk about prevenient grace without using technical terms.

pastorals.body:
For those who feel they have to ’do something’ to stay in God’s good books, this chapter is incredibly liberating. Watch for the ’relief’ that comes when people realize the work is finished.


However, some may find the idea of God choosing us (John 6:37, 44) difficult or even offensive. Don’t get bogged down in deep theological debates about election; keep the focus on the comfort and humility it produces.

watch.body: 👁️ Watch for the person who says ’this is too easy.’ They might be missing the cost Christ paid. Reiterate that while grace is free for us, it was infinitely expensive for him.

keyPoints.body:
These are the two sides of the same coin. You can’t have one without the other. Repentance is turning from; Faith is turning to.


Distinguish between godly grief and worldly grief (2 Corinthians 7:9-10). Worldly grief is about the consequences of sin; godly grief is about the offence of sin against God.


Clarify that faith isn’t about the strength of our belief, but the object of our belief. A weak hand holding a strong rope is still safe.

pastorals.body:
Some people struggle with perpetual repentance — they keep repenting for the same thing without ever moving into the rest of faith. They need to hear that repentance is a turn, not a treadmill.


Others may have ’easy believism’ where they claim faith but have never actually turned from sin. The James 2-style emphasis (though not in this chapter’s verses, it’s the underlying theme) is important here: real faith produces change.

watch.body: 👁️ Watch for the ’Tom’ in your group (from the intro) — the person who is sorry but hasn’t actually made the call. Encourage a specific, practical response.

keyPoints.body:
This chapter deepens the understanding of regeneration. It’s the ’engine room’ of conversion. God doesn’t just give us a new record (justification); he gives us a new heart (Ezekiel 36:26).


The Ezekiel 37 (Dry Bones) passage is a powerful visual of the Spirit’s work. It reinforces that we are entirely dependent on God for spiritual life.


Help the group see that new desires are the primary evidence of new birth. We start to love what we once hated and hate what we once loved.

pastorals.body:
This is a great chapter for those who feel ’stuck’ in their Christian life. If they have new life, the growth is inevitable, even if it’s slow. Encourage them to look for the signs of life (new desires, new love for God’s word) rather than just a perfect performance.


Watch for the person who feels they don’t have these new desires. Use it as an opportunity to pray for the Spirit’s work, rather than just telling them to try harder.

watch.body: 👁️ Watch for those who get frustrated that they aren’t ’perfect’ yet. Remind them that a baby is fully alive from day one, but it still has a lot of growing to do.

keyPoints.body:
This session challenges the privatisation of faith. Conversion isn’t just about me and God; it’s about me, God, and the body of Christ.


The body/stone imagery (1 Cor 12, 1 Peter 2) shows that our individual identity is now wrapped up in a communal one. We belong to each other.


Address the responsibility we have to one another. Church isn’t a building we visit; it’s a family we belong to, which includes mutual accountability (Hebrews 10:24-25).

pastorals.body:
Many people have ’church baggage.’ They’ve been hurt, bored, or disillusioned by the local church. Acknowledge the messiness of church while holding onto the biblical vision. Don’t let ’the church is perfect’ be the message; the message is ’the church is God’s family, and he loves it.’


For the ’loner’ Christian, this chapter can be very challenging. Be prepared to talk through why we find belonging so hard (vulnerability, cost, time).

watch.body: 👁️ Watch for the ’Marcus’ in your group — the one who is content to stay private. The goal is to move them toward visible, committed belonging.

keyPoints.body:
This chapter deals with sanctification. Conversion isn’t just a past event; it’s a present reality that produces fruit (Galatians 5:22-23).


The conflict in Galatians 5:16-17 is crucial. Being a Christian doesn’t mean the struggle with sin ends; it means the rule of sin ends. The struggle is actually a sign of life.


Emphasise love as the motive for obedience (John 14:21). We don’t obey to get God to love us; we obey because he already does.

pastorals.body:
There is a fine line here between encouraging holiness and falling back into legalism. Keep pointing back to the Spirit as the source of the fruit. We don’t ’manufacture’ fruit; we ’bear’ it as we stay connected to Christ.


Be ready to comfort the person who feels they have very little fruit. Help them look for the ’small’ fruit — a moment of patience, a quickness to repent — that they might be overlooking.

watch.body: 👁️ Watch for the person who uses ’I’m not perfect’ as an excuse for not pursuing holiness. The goal is growth, not just being comfortable with sin.

keyPoints.body:
This is a dual-track chapter: comfort for the true believer and warning for the self-deceived.


Assurance is grounded in the objective work of Christ (Hebrews 10:11-14) and confirmed by the subjective work of the Spirit and a changed life (Romans 8:15-16, 1 John 2:3-5).


The warning passages (Matthew 7:21-23, Titus 1:16) are not meant to make the true believer anxious, but to wake up the person who has a ’dead faith.’

pastorals.body:
This can be the most sensitive chapter of all. You will likely have people on both sides: the anxious person who needs comfort, and the complacent person who needs a wake-up call. Your job is to try to give each person what they need.


If someone is deeply unsettled by this chapter, spend time with them afterwards. Help them look at their life for signs of life (even tiny ones) rather than perfection.

watch.body: 👁️ Watch for the person who is too sure of themselves but shows no fruit — they need the warning. And watch for the person who is never sure despite clear fruit — they need the comfort.

keyPoints.body:
The final chapter reframes evangelism away from ’salesmanship’ and toward faithful proclamation.


The liberating point: conversion is God’s work, so the pressure is off the evangelist. We cannot open hearts or produce new birth. We speak clearly and leave the outcome with God.


Focus on the content (Christ alone) and the manner (gentleness, patience, clarity) of our sharing.

pastorals.body:
Watch for people from church cultures where high-pressure evangelism was normalised. This chapter’s emphasis on honest, non-manipulative proclamation may come as significant relief – and may also require some gentle reprocessing of past experiences.


Also watch for those so relieved by ’results are God’s’ that they use it as a reason never to speak at all. The closing challenge – ‘Who is the person God has put in front of you?’ – gently closes that escape route.

watch.body: 👁️ The final question asks people to name a specific person. Try to make sure the group actually answers it – not as a performance but as a genuine act of prayerful attention. It may be the most practically significant moment of the whole nine sessions.

paras: Rick Creighton grew up in Northern Ireland and studied maths at Oxford and then taught maths at a secondary school in England. After that, he studied theology at Moore Theological College in Sydney and London School of Theology.

He served as a church pastor in England for ten years, before moving to Nigeria in 2012, where he has been teaching at ECWA Theological Seminary Kagoro and Jos ECWA Theological Seminary. He has been involved with BeaconLight Training since 2003.
Rick is married to Alanna. They have three children: Asha, Conor, and Jack.

publisher.name: BeaconLight
paras: BeaconLight is a non-denominational Christian training organisation based in the UK, founded in 1995. Its aim is to equip Christians worldwide with practically-applied Biblical gospel ministry tools — resources designed to help people both equip themselves and equip others.

BeaconLight’s resources include CrossCheck, which enables Christians to explain their faith and lead others to Jesus, and Word@Work, a verse-by-verse applied devotional commentary of the whole New Testament. BeaconLight also provides printed Biblical resources to prisons, seafarers, police, and the armed services.
BeaconLight believes that Christian ministry should be free of charge, and all its online materials are available without registration or payment. The ministry is funded entirely by donations.
BeaconLight is a member of the Evangelical Alliance and Global Connections, and is a registered charity in England and Wales (Charity No. 1047046).
